meta.title: Enterprise AI Architecture Principles (AWS GenAI Lens — Aligned Catalogue)
meta.description: Concrete, enforceable principles that concretise AWS Well-Architected Generative AI Lens best practices. Each principle names who validates it, when it is checked, and what artefacts the validator inspects. Catalogue rebuilt from scratch on the v1.5 schema; mappings are fetched from the live AWS Lens, not inherited from the legacy catalogue.
meta.author: Dheeraj
notes: This catalogue is being built one principle at a time, BP by BP, against the live AWS GenAI Lens. The legacy catalogue lives at `principles_old.json` and is retained for reference but is not authoritative for this rebuild.

Every principle in this catalogue must concretise (or explicitly extend) an AWS GenAI Lens BP, with the BP confirmed against the source documentation at https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/generative-ai-lens/. The verification date is recorded on each reference's `last_checked` field.

Principles anchor at the AWS implementation-step level by default, not the BP level. The per-BP due-diligence record (which steps were promoted to principles, which were not, and why) lives in `lens_mapping.md`. See `taxonomy.json` framework_mappings_spec.anchoring_methodology.

Pillars and focus areas in this catalogue mirror the AWS GenAI Lens vocabulary (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization). Sustainability — the sixth AWS pillar — is omitted; sustainability concerns are distributed across Cost Optimization, Operational Excellence, and Security.

Schema is vendor-neutral. `framework_mappings` is keyed by framework (`aws`, `azure`, `gcp`, …); the catalogue currently populates `aws` only. Azure / GCP mappings can be added per principle without schema change.

Schema v1.6 (2026-05-26) re-introduced two root-level fields on every principle: `applicability` (which AI patterns the principle applies to — llm/rag/agentic/ml) and `maturity_level` (when an adopting organisation should care — foundational/scaling/mature). PRIN_001 and PRIN_002 were retrofitted in the same session.

Schema v1.7 (2026-05-26, same day as v1.6) reshaped `applicability` from a flat array of patterns to a pattern-criticality map: legacy `['agentic']` becomes `{ 'agentic': 'mandatory' }`. New `enums.pattern_criticality` (mandatory / nice_to_have) carries the criticality per pattern. PRIN_001 and PRIN_002 retrofitted; both kept narrow (agentic-only). See taxonomy.json conventions.choosing_pattern_criticality.

Schema v1.8 (2026-05-26, same day as v1.6 and v1.7) added an optional `explain_prompt` field on every principle. The field holds a compiled OpenAI-style system + user_template pair used by the runtime UI's Problem-tab 'Explain' affordance to generate illustrative examples for a chosen industry and implementation type. Compiled prompts are principle-specific so the prompt can honour each principle's failure shape (silent regression, setup absence, cumulative drift, one-shot breach) without a single universal prompt distorting the narrative. PRIN_001, PRIN_002, and PRIN_003 compiled in-session. See taxonomy.json conventions.per_principle_explain_prompt_compilation and the schema-v1.8 entry in decisions.md.

Principle ID rename (2026-05-27): the four principles were renamed from the legacy `PRIN_NNN` format to the new BP-anchored, sequential format `<bp_code>-NN`. PRIN_001 → GO1B1-01, PRIN_002 → GO1B1-02, PRIN_003 → GO1B1-03, PRIN_004 → GO1B1-04. Each principle received a PATCH bump on its change_history. The `bp_code` compresses the AWS pillar / question / BP triple to 5 characters (GO = GenOps, 1 = Q1, B = BP marker, 1 = BP1); the `NN` suffix is a sequential counter — the Nth principle promoted from that BP, in promotion order. IDs within a BP are consecutive (01, 02, 03, …) with no gaps, regardless of which AWS implementation steps were not_promoted. The AWS step a principle concretises is recorded in framework_mappings.aws.references[0].implementation_step.number rather than encoded in the ID suffix. The taxonomy convention is at taxonomy.json conventions.principle_id_format; the rename rationale is in the 2026-05-27 entry in decisions.md. Historical references in append-only journals (decisions.md prose, prior change_history summaries) were intentionally left under the legacy IDs; only live cross-references were updated.

Schema v1.9 (2026-05-29) added a required root-level `serving_paradigm` field on every principle — a criticality map (same shape as `applicability` v1.7) keyed by foundation-model serving paradigm. Enum values: api_on_demand / api_provisioned / self_hosted_managed / self_hosted_unmanaged. Surfaced during the GENCOST02-BP02 walkthrough where the candidate GC2B2-01 principle (right-size hosting infrastructure) is intrinsically self-hosting-only — applicability could not express the narrowing because it is keyed by AI pattern (llm/rag/agentic/ml), not by hosting model. All six pre-existing principles (GO1B1-01..05 + GC1B1-01) were retrofitted with `{ api_on_demand: mandatory, api_provisioned: mandatory, self_hosted_managed: mandatory, self_hosted_unmanaged: mandatory }` — every existing principle's discipline (eval harness, stratification, metric encapsulation, drift monitor, refresh cadence, model-selection ADR) applies regardless of how the model is served. Each principle received a MINOR change_history bump. See taxonomy.json conventions.choosing_serving_paradigm and the 2026-05-29 schema v1.9 entry in decisions.md for the rationale.

references.question: GENOPS01 — How do you achieve and verify consistent model output quality?

implementation_step.title: Create a ground truth dataset
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Create a ground truth dataset. Verify that you have diverse data representation. Consider various user personas and use cases.

references.note: AWS step 1 calls for the dataset to exist with diverse representation across user personas and use cases. AWS leaves unspecified: where the dataset lives, who owns it, what shape it takes for an autonomous agent (input + expected decision + expected evidence trace), and how strata are declared and verified. This principle concretises all four.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.A: Govern Data for AI Systems
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — the eval harness is a curated data artefact governing model evaluation, which aligns with III.A's data-governance scope. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.A competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Maintain a versioned ground-truth evaluation harness for agent decisions in the workload repository
statement.description: Each agent workload must establish a ground-truth evaluation harness, committed to the workload repository, with each entry structured to capture the input payload, the expected decision, and the expected evidence trace. The harness must include diverse representation of production scenarios — personas, use cases, and input variations the workload sees in real use.

problem.description: Without a version-controlled ground-truth harness that can be re-run on every code or configuration change to the agent, changes ship to production with unknown regression risk. There is no mechanical way to block a bad migration — only post-deployment discovery via customer impact. The harness must be part of the workload repository so every developer can run it, every CI build invokes it, and no architect's local notebook becomes a single point of failure.
examples: Original architect hand-validated production scenarios on their laptop and ran them ad-hoc; the harness was never committed to the repository; when the architect left, no one could re-run it on the next change.
Harness exists in the repository but only captures inputs, not expected outputs; CI can run the agent against each scenario but has nothing to compare its decisions against.

Harness captures the expected decision but not the expected evidence trace; an agent that stops consulting a key signal still produces the right output for the wrong reason, and the regression is invisible.
Harness entries have inconsistent free-form schema; the CI validator silently skips malformed entries, so coverage looks complete but isn't.

solution.approach: Commit the test harness to the workload repository at a known path (convention: `eval/`, with `eval/scenarios/` for labelled test cases and `eval/data/` for supporting input data). Wire the harness into the CI pipeline so it runs on every PR that changes agent code or configuration. Migration to production is blocked when the harness fails.
key_benefits: Foundational dependency for periodic evaluation (GENOPS01-BP01 step 3), drift monitoring (step 6), and harness refresh (step 7) — none of those can run without this principle in place.
Production knowledge survives team changes; the harness is in the repository, not in any individual's head or local notebook.

Structured schema (input + expected decision + expected evidence) lets the evaluation pipeline grade both decisions AND reasoning paths — catches the failure mode where the output is right but for the wrong reason.
Re-labelling work is bounded — entries are first-class repository artefacts with diff tooling for label changes, not opaque blobs.

gates.check: On any PR, both `eval/scenarios/` and `eval/data/` must exist in the repository and be non-empty. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that touches files under `src/agent/` (agent code or configuration), the test harness in `eval/` must run against the proposed changes and all tests must pass. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge and stalls migration to production.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-01 — Maintain a versioned ground-truth evaluation harness for agent decisions in the workload repository — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every agent workload must maintain a ground-truth evaluation harness, committed to the workload repository, where each entry captures the input payload, the expected decision, and the expected evidence trace. The harness must include diverse representation of production scenarios — personas, use cases, input variations. It must be runnable by anyone on the team and wired to CI so it runs on every change to agent code or configuration.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible the moment the workload needs to evolve. The team has an agent in production that appears to be working. Validation was done by the original architect locally — in a notebook, ad-hoc, or in a script that 'works on my machine' but was never committed to the repository. The team is confident because the agent has been running. The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: a change ships that the team cannot reliably validate; the original architect leaves and nobody can re-run the validation; a regression hits production and nobody can reproduce it; an audit or handover asks for the evaluation suite and there isn't one. At that moment the team discovers that production knowledge lived in one head, on one laptop, or in a notebook that has since drifted. The consequence is a forced choice: ship the change blind, rebuild the suite from memory, or stall the workload while the discipline is retrofitted.

The principle's value is forcing the harness to be a first-class repository artefact from day one — committed, runnable by anyone, wired to CI. Without it, every change ships with unknown regression risk and validation discipline cannot survive personnel change.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-01 is mandatory for agent (autonomous, tool-using, multi-step) workloads. If the chosen Implementation type is not 'Agent', open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for autonomous agents and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest in the chosen pattern.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload that is in production and that the team believes is operating correctly. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the role the workload plays. Crucially: name how the team thinks they have validated the workload — the informal mechanism (the original architect runs three scenarios in a notebook before each release; the lead developer maintains a local script that exercises the main decision branches; the team keeps a Slack thread of test cases pasted into a dev console pre-release). The reader should picture a confident team unaware that the discipline does not survive contact with change. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the absent harness becomes visible. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the triggering event (a refactor that needs validation; the original architect moves to another team; a customer-reported regression that needs reproduction; an audit, handover, or onboarding ask for the suite); then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart. Name roles (the duty engineer on call; the new senior joining to inherit the workload). Make the consequence concrete — production exposure, time spent rebuilding the suite from memory, a regression that ships because nobody could reliably re-validate, or a stall while the harness is retrofitted. The failure must demonstrate something a harness committed to the repository with CI wiring would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'the model performed badly' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly two weeks', 'about a dozen scenarios').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

dependencies.reason: Stratifies and gates GO1B1-01's ground-truth harness; there is nothing to stratify until the harness exists.

references.question: GENOPS01 — How do you achieve and verify consistent model output quality?

implementation_step.title: Apply stratified sampling techniques
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Apply stratified sampling techniques. Categorize ground truth data into relevant groups. Randomly sample from each group to achieve balanced representation.

references.note: AWS step 2 calls for the evaluation dataset to be split into relevant categories (the named example is user personas) with sampling balanced across them. AWS leaves unspecified: where the strata are declared, how they are enforced, what minimum size each stratum must have, how per-stratum metrics are reported and gated, and what counts as an architecturally meaningful stratum for an autonomous agent (decision branches, tool-call paths, edge-case policy carve-outs). This principle concretises all five and ties stratification to per-stratum CI gating rather than aggregate pass/fail.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.A: Govern Data for AI Systems
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — strata declaration and per-stratum minima are data-governance disciplines on the evaluation dataset, aligning with III.A's data-governance scope. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.A competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Stratify the ground-truth harness and gate evaluation per stratum, not on the aggregate
statement.description: The ground-truth evaluation harness established under GO1B1-01 must declare its strata as a versioned artefact in the workload repository, with a minimum example count per stratum and a per-stratum metric threshold. The harness must emit per-stratum metrics on every run, and the CI gate must fail when any stratum is missing the minimum example count or fails its per-stratum threshold — not only when an aggregate pass-rate falls below a global threshold.

problem.description: Without stratified composition and per-stratum gating, an aggregate pass-rate can sit at green while a small but business-critical subset of agent behaviour silently regresses. The failure surfaces in production, not in CI, because the eval set was never deliberately constructed to test the minority class with statistical weight. Strata for an agentic workload are domain-specific (decision branches, tool-call paths, persona segments, policy carve-outs) and cannot be inferred mechanically — they must be declared. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: Returns triage agent passes 95% of an evaluation set of 100 scenarios; the set was assembled from a random sample of production traffic, so 90 of the 100 are AUTO_APPROVE and only 1 is REJECT — REJECT accuracy is statistically unmeasurable and a policy regression ships unnoticed.

Agent has three decision branches (AUTO_APPROVE / HUMAN_REVIEW / REJECT) but harness entries carry no stratum field; the team cannot answer 'how did REJECT accuracy change in this PR' even though aggregate accuracy is reported.
Strata manifest exists but declares only one stratum, 'all_returns', defeating the discipline; the CI check passes because the manifest is non-empty.
Strata are populated but the harness only emits an aggregate metric; per-stratum regression is invisible until a customer complains.

New decision branch added to the agent in a PR; no scenarios added to cover it; strata manifest not updated; aggregate accuracy unchanged because the new branch is rarely hit in eval; the branch ships untested.

solution.approach: Declare strata in a versioned manifest at a known repository path (convention: `eval/strata.yaml`), each stratum naming its identifier, its rationale, its minimum example count, and its per-stratum metric threshold. Every entry in `eval/scenarios/` carries a `stratum` field pointing at one declared stratum. The evaluation harness emits per-stratum metrics on every run and exits non-zero when any stratum is below its minimum count or fails its per-stratum threshold. The strata manifest is reviewed by the Project Architect at every release as part of self-attestation; the PA confirms the strata still reflect the agent's decision branches, tool paths, and policy carve-outs.
key_benefits: Per-stratum gating catches the minority-class regression that aggregate metrics hide — the dominant production failure mode for agent decisioning systems with skewed traffic.
Strata are first-class versioned artefacts, so any change to them is reviewed in a PR; the strata grow with the agent rather than living in the architect's head.
Coupling strata to decision branches and tool paths makes the eval set's architectural coverage queryable: a reader can answer 'is every decision branch tested' from the manifest.
Builds on GO1B1-01 without replacing it: GO1B1-01 establishes that the harness exists with structured entries; GO1B1-02 constrains how the harness is composed and how its output is gated.

gates.check: On any PR, `eval/strata.yaml` must exist and declare at least one stratum; every entry in `eval/scenarios/` must carry a `stratum` field referencing a declared stratum; every declared stratum must have at least its declared minimum example count present in `eval/scenarios/`; the harness run must emit per-stratum metrics and every stratum must meet its declared per-stratum threshold. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that touches files under `src/agent/` and adds or alters a decision branch, tool integration, or policy carve-out, the PR must also modify `eval/strata.yaml` OR include a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why no strata change is needed. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-02 — Stratify the ground-truth harness and gate evaluation per stratum, not on the aggregate — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

The principle requires the agent's evaluation harness to declare its strata as a versioned artefact in the workload repository, with a minimum example count per stratum and a per-stratum metric threshold. The harness must emit per-stratum metrics on every run, and CI must fail when any stratum is below its minimum count or fails its per-stratum threshold — not only when an aggregate pass-rate falls below a global threshold. Strata for an agent are typically decision branches, tool-call paths, persona segments, and policy carve-outs.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a silent-regression failure that hides behind a healthy aggregate. The team has a working evaluation harness (PRIN_001 is satisfied). Scenarios run on every PR; the aggregate pass-rate is reported and sits comfortably in the green. The eval set was assembled at some point in the past from a random sample of production traffic, so it inherits production's skew — the dominant decision class is heavily represented, the minority classes are not. A change ships that subtly regresses one of the minority classes. The aggregate barely moves because the minority class is too small to shift it. The regression sits in production, invisible to CI, until some other mechanism surfaces it: a customer complaint about a specific kind of case, a quarterly business review that segments outcomes by category, an audit sample, a compliance check on a small but consequential class.

The principle's value is forcing per-stratum visibility and per-stratum gating, so a regression in any deliberately-named slice of the agent's behaviour cannot pass CI on the strength of a healthy aggregate. The strata are named by the team because they cannot be inferred mechanically — decision branches, tool-call paths, policy carve-outs, and persona segments are domain-specific.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-02 is mandatory for agent workloads. If the chosen Implementation type is not 'Agent', open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for autonomous agents (where the strata vocabulary — decision branches, tool-call paths, policy carve-outs — is sharpest) and that the example below is illustrative of how the same minority-class-regression failure would manifest in the chosen pattern.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-02's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload in production with a known and stable mix of outputs across multiple categories — a dominant majority class and one or more business-critical minorities. Be specific about the industry and implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Crucially: name the decision categories the workload produces (for a returns triage agent: AUTO_APPROVE / HUMAN_REVIEW / REJECT) and their rough mix (AUTO_APPROVE dominates daily volume; REJECT is rare but every wrong REJECT becomes a customer dispute). Make clear that the team has an evaluation harness and monitors aggregate accuracy on every PR. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe a regression in the minority class that the aggregate metric does not flinch on. Walk through the scenario chronologically: a change ships (a prompt revision, a tool addition, a model upgrade, a fine-tune retrain); the harness runs in CI and aggregate accuracy is unchanged or even slightly up; the change merges. The regression sits silently in production until something other than CI surfaces it — a customer complaint about a specific class of decision, a quarterly business review that segments outcomes by category, an audit sample. Name roles (the duty AP clerk; the head of category ops; the duty analyst handling escalations). Make the consequence concrete — disputes, refunded customers, compliance escalations, manual remediation effort. The failure must demonstrate something per-stratum gating would have caught at PR time and aggregate gating would not — not a generic 'the model got worse' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The returns triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 4% of daily volume', 'about a fortnight before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

implementation_step.verbatim_text: Define custom metrics. Use the fmeval library to create custom metric classes. Encapsulate logic for calculating specific evaluation criteria.

references.note: AWS step 4 calls for custom metric classes that encapsulate evaluation criteria, expressed via the fmeval library. The fmeval reference is vendor-specific; the architectural content sits one layer up — metric definitions are encapsulated code units (a named class or function in its own file with a stable interface), not inline expressions in eval scripts or notebooks. AWS leaves unspecified: where metric code lives in the repository, what interface metrics must expose, whether the eval runner may contain inline scoring logic, and how metric changes are reviewed. This principle concretises all four — a fixed directory convention (`eval/metrics/`), a stable interface convention, a no-inline-logic constraint on the runner, and metric changes as first-class reviewable diffs in their own files.

statement.title: Implement every evaluation metric as a named, encapsulated code unit
statement.description: Each evaluation metric used by the workload's harness must be implemented as a named, encapsulated unit of code — a class or function with a stable interface — in a known directory of the workload repository (convention: `eval/metrics/`). The evaluation runner imports metrics from this directory and contains no inline scoring logic. Each metric is independently unit-testable.

problem.description: Without encapsulation, evaluation metrics live as inline expressions in eval scripts or notebooks, scattered across files, or duplicated across projects. The same metric ends up with slightly different definitions in different places. Metric changes happen invisibly because they are buried in eval-runner diffs and cannot be reviewed cleanly. Metrics cannot be unit-tested on their own because they are not callable units. Reuse across projects becomes copy-paste, and the copies subsequently diverge. The team eventually cannot trust its own eval numbers — two runs of the 'same metric' produce different scores, an onboarding hire cannot tell what `tone_score` actually measures, and a senior reviewer cannot approve a metric change because the diff is hidden inside a 400-line runner-file refactor. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: Two developers tweak the same `tone_correctness` metric in parallel PRs, each editing their own inline copy in the eval runner; both PRs pass review because the diffs look small; the two versions silently diverge in how they treat borderline phrases, and downstream metric values are no longer comparable across the codebase.

A 400-line `evaluate.py` mixes scoring logic and orchestration; a refactor PR's diff is dominated by orchestration changes and quietly rewrites a metric definition in passing; the metric change ships unreviewed because nobody in the PR review noticed the three-line semantic shift inside a wall of refactoring.

A new joiner inherits the eval pipeline and asks 'what does `decision_correctness` actually check?'; the lead data scientist cannot answer without re-reading fifty lines of `evaluate.py`; onboarding stalls for the better part of a week while the new joiner reconstructs the implicit metric semantics from Git history.

A metric defined inline in `evaluate.py` is copy-pasted into a sister project's notebook; a bugfix to one copy never reaches the other; six months later the two projects' eval numbers diverge and nobody can identify which definition is canonical.

Eval results stop replicating between the local dev environment and CI because the inline metric implementations have drifted across branches and worktrees; the team can no longer attribute score changes to model changes versus eval-code changes.

solution.approach: Create a directory `eval/metrics/` under the workload repository. Each metric is its own file, exporting a named class (or function) with a stable interface — for example a Python class with an `evaluate(output, expected) -> float` method, or the team's equivalent convention in their chosen language. Each metric file has a sibling test (under `eval/metrics/tests/` or co-located) covering at least the basic positive and negative cases. The evaluation runner imports metric instances from `eval/metrics/` and iterates over them; the runner module contains no scoring logic of its own — only orchestration, input loading, output collection, and reporting. New metrics, edits to existing metrics, and metric removals are reviewed as ordinary code changes with diffs visible in their own files.
key_benefits: Metric changes appear as discrete diffs in their own files; a reviewer can focus on the semantics of a single metric in isolation rather than fishing it out of a large runner-file diff.
Metrics are unit-testable; a semantic regression in a metric is caught by that metric's own tests before it can affect any eval run.
Metrics are reusable across projects and across eval runs without copy-paste; a fix to a metric definition propagates everywhere via normal import semantics.
Onboarding becomes tractable: a new joiner reads the metric files to understand what is being measured rather than reconstructing meaning from runner code.
The eval runner becomes short, dumb, and stable; runner changes are unambiguously infrastructure changes, not metric-semantics changes hiding behind orchestration.

gates.check: On any PR, `eval/metrics/` must exist and contain at least one named, encapsulated metric — a class or function with a stable interface (e.g. an `evaluate(output, expected)` method, or the team's equivalent in their chosen language). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that touches files under `eval/`, no metric definitions may appear outside `eval/metrics/` — the evaluation runner imports metrics, never defines them inline. Mechanically: a lint that fails if a class matching the team's `*Metric` naming convention (or an equivalent metric-shaped function signature) is defined in any file outside `eval/metrics/`. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENOPS01-BP01 implementation step 4 (Define custom metrics). Third principle in the catalogue under the step-level anchoring methodology; first principle authored natively to the v1.7 schema (PRIN_001 and PRIN_002 were retrofitted). Scope: metrics declared as code at `eval/metrics/`, metric names resolvable from `eval/scenarios/` and `eval/strata.yaml`, per-metric harness output, per-metric per-stratum gating, and a coverage gate tying agent-surface changes (new tools, output-shape changes, policy carve-outs) to metric changes or ADRs. AWS's fmeval-specific sub-bullets are stripped — the architectural content (metric definitions are encapsulated, named, version-controlled code) sits one layer up and is vendor-neutral. Builds on PRIN_001 (harness existence) and PRIN_002 (strata + per-stratum gating); PRIN_003 closes the hole where a team could comply with both by declaring a single boolean metric whose semantics are unstated. The strata.yaml shape evolves from a single `threshold` per stratum to a `thresholds: { metric_name: value }` map; PRIN_002's wording (`per-stratum metric threshold`) is read as generic-enough to accommodate this refinement without a PRIN_002 content change — flagged as an open item in case the user wants PRIN_002 bumped to v1.3.0 to make the per-metric shape explicit. Project-tier; PA self-attests metric-set meaningfulness at release; mechanical compliance enforced by CI gates as required status checks on the integration branch. applicability: { 'agentic': 'mandatory' } — kept narrow consistent with PRIN_001 and PRIN_002; sibling principles for RAG / LLM / ML deferred until those subject areas are walked through. maturity_level: 'foundational' — the failure mode lands on day one of any non-trivial agent workload. AIGP cross-reference set to Domain III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)) with mapping_state: 'unverified' pending side-by-side competency review; distinct from PRIN_001 and PRIN_002's III.A anchoring because metric definition is a model-lifecycle design activity rather than data governance.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: MAJOR rewrite against what AWS step 4 actually says (`Define custom metrics. Use the fmeval library to create custom metric classes. Encapsulate logic for calculating specific evaluation criteria.`). The v1.x line had built an elaborate 'silent regression of a verification chain' / 'right answer for wrong reason' / 'soft synthesis' failure-mode theory around the step. Field tests on Retail + Agent (InvoiceWizard), Airline + Agent (FareRateService), Fashion + Agent (ChicAdvisor), and Insurance + Agent (ClaimCheck / SecureLife medical records) repeatedly failed to support that theory — the generations were either self-contradictory, dependent on visibly bad system design, or did not demonstrate a failure that simpler controls would miss. The author surfaced the diagnosis directly: the step itself is a plain software-engineering hygiene rule about putting evaluation metrics in named, encapsulated code (a class or function with a stable interface) in their own directory, rather than as inline expressions in eval scripts or notebooks. v2.0.0 rewrites the principle against that honest reading. Statement now: 'Encapsulated Evaluation Metrics in the Workload Repository.' Problem now: inline metric definitions drift across PRs, are buried in runner-file diffs and so cannot be reviewed cleanly, are not callable units and so cannot be unit-tested, and end up copy-pasted across projects where they subsequently diverge. Solution now: `eval/metrics/` directory with one metric per file, a stable interface convention, sibling tests, and an eval runner that contains no scoring logic. Gates now: (1) `eval/metrics/` exists with at least one named, encapsulated metric (required status check); (2) no metric definitions live outside `eval/metrics/` — lint catches metric-shaped classes/functions defined elsewhere (required status check). Both gates are project-tier and CI-enforced. Applicability broadens from agentic-only to agent + RAG + LLM mandatory, ML nice_to_have — the metrics-as-code discipline applies wherever a team writes custom evaluation logic; for classical ML, standard library metrics (sklearn.metrics, etc.) typically already carry the discipline. Maturity remains foundational. AIGP cross-reference remains III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)) — encapsulating evaluation metrics is a build-phase design discipline. compiled_for_principle_version bumped to 2.0.0 with the explain_prompt totally rewritten to a cumulative-drift / code-hygiene failure shape (inline definitions drifting over months across PRs and notebooks; metric semantics changes shipping unreviewed inside runner refactors; team losing trust in its own eval numbers; onboarding becoming archaeology). The previous v1.x failure-mode theory is now documented as scope-creep that the catalogue is honest about, not pretended away. See the v2.0.0 entry in decisions.md.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-03 — Implement every evaluation metric as a named, encapsulated code unit — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every evaluation metric used by the workload's harness must be implemented as a named, encapsulated unit of code — a class or function with a stable interface — in a known directory of the workload repository (convention: `eval/metrics/`). The evaluation runner imports metrics from this directory and contains no inline scoring logic. Each metric is independently unit-testable. This is software-engineering hygiene for evaluation: metrics are first-class code objects, not inline expressions in eval scripts or notebooks.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a code-hygiene failure that compounds over time. The team is running evaluations — that part works. But the metric calculations live inline in eval scripts, scattered across notebooks, or duplicated across files. As the eval suite grows, the inline definitions silently drift: one PR tweaks a metric's threshold, another PR copy-pastes the metric into a new context and edits it slightly, a third PR refactors the eval runner and quietly rewrites a metric definition in passing. Nobody catches these because the changes do not appear as discrete diffs to a named metric — they are hidden inside large eval-runner files.

Over months the costs accumulate. Two runs of the 'same metric' produce different scores. The team cannot reproduce last quarter's eval results. A new joiner cannot tell what `tone_score` actually checks without an archaeology session through Git history. A metric used in two projects has long since diverged between them. Unit tests for metric logic do not exist because the metrics are not isolated units that can be tested. Eventually the team stops trusting its own eval numbers, and an audit or onboarding event forces a reckoning — a substantial rebuild of the eval setup follows.

The principle's value is the boring software-engineering hygiene that turns metrics into first-class code objects: named, encapsulated, importable, testable, reviewable. Nothing fancy — just write your metrics properly, from the start.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-03 is mandatory for agent, RAG, and LLM-Only workloads — anywhere the team writes custom evaluation logic. It is nice-to-have for classical ML workloads, where standard library metrics (sklearn.metrics et al.) typically already carry the encapsulation discipline. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting that PRIN_003 is nice-to-have rather than mandatory for ML workloads because library-provided metrics usually already encapsulate the discipline; the example below is illustrative of how the failure would manifest if the team had custom ML metrics not covered by the library.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-03's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a team in production that is running evaluation on their AI workload. Be specific about the industry and implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Crucially: describe how the team currently handles their evaluation metrics — name the inline pattern. Examples: 'the eval script is a 400-line evaluate.py with three big functions, each doing different scoring inline'; 'metric calculations live in a Jupyter notebook the lead data scientist maintains; the team copy-pastes cells into a CI script before each release'; 'each developer has their own fork of metrics.py with slightly different inline scoring expressions'. Make clear the team IS evaluating (this is not a no-eval failure) — they just do not treat metrics as first-class code. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe how the inline-metric pattern leads to a concrete failure that encapsulation would have prevented. Pick one or two of these patterns: two developers tweak the same metric in parallel PRs and ship subtly different versions because neither saw the other's change; a metric definition is rewritten in passing during an eval-runner refactor and ships unreviewed because the diff was dominated by orchestration changes; a new joiner asks 'what does tone_score actually check?' and nobody can answer without re-reading fifty lines of runner code; eval results stop replicating between local dev and CI because inline implementations have drifted; a metric copy-pasted into a sister project diverges silently from its original. Walk through chronologically. Name roles (the lead data scientist; the new ML platform hire; the on-call engineer). Make the consequence concrete — distrust of the eval pipeline, a back-test of eval results, weeks of cleanup work to extract metrics into proper modules, an audit finding about reproducibility. The failure must be a maintainability / reviewability / testability failure of the eval setup itself, not a runtime model failure, not a regression of the workload's outputs."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name files, scripts, line counts, roles. 'The 400-line evaluate.py' not 'the eval pipeline'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 400 lines', 'about a fortnight of cleanup').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only.
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only — the discipline that should have been in place but was not.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

dependencies.reason: Compares production metrics against the deployment baseline GO1B1-01's harness produces — no baseline, nothing to detect drift against.
Per-stratum drift detection reuses GO1B1-02's declared strata when present, but drift can be watched on the aggregate harness without them.
Runs GO1B1-03's encapsulated metric units on a cadence rather than re-implementing scoring logic.

references.question: GENOPS01 — How do you achieve and verify consistent model output quality?

implementation_step.title: Monitor for performance drifts
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Monitor for performance drifts. Identify potential degradation in model performance. Address data drift where actual usage diverges from the initial ground truth.

references.note: AWS step 6 names two distinct drift dimensions — model performance degradation (output drift) and actual-usage divergence from the initial ground truth (input drift). AWS leaves unspecified: where the monitor's configuration lives, how cadence is declared, what baseline shape is required, how alerts are routed, and what happens when the model is updated (potential baseline staleness). This principle concretises all five — `eval/drift/config.yaml` as the config artefact, per-section per-dimension cadence and thresholds, per-dimension baselines anchored to the deployment, alert_routing in-config, and a baseline-update coupling gate (Gate 2).

statement.title: Monitor production inputs and model outputs for drift against the deployment baseline
statement.description: Each deployed workload must operate a scheduled drift monitor. The monitor compares (a) current model evaluation metrics against the baseline established at deployment, and (b) the distribution of production inputs against the input distribution of the ground-truth harness. Its cadence and thresholds are declared in `eval/drift/config.yaml`; the monitor emits an alert when either dimension crosses a threshold.

problem.description: Without a scheduled drift monitor comparing production outputs and inputs against the deployment baseline, an AI workload's performance can degrade silently over weeks or months. Per-PR CI gates (GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03) catch regressions a specific PR introduces, but they cannot catch drift that emerges from shifting production traffic patterns or from compounding small effects in deployed model behaviour. The failure typically surfaces only via something external — a quarterly review, a customer escalation, an audit — by which point the model has been silently underperforming for weeks. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A claims-triage agent's REJECT-rate baseline at deployment was 8%; six months in, the rate has drifted to 14% because production claim mix shifted (more high-value edge cases); no PR-time gate fires because no PR changed the agent; the drift surfaces only in the quarterly business review.

A returns-triage agent was harnessed against Q1 patterns; by Q4 seasonal shift has moved input distribution significantly; AUTO_APPROVE accuracy slipped from 94% to 88%; GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 gates still pass per-PR because the harness itself hasn't changed.

A fraud-scoring model's precision was 0.92 at baseline; over 8 months, fraud patterns evolved and precision slipped to 0.81; no single week showed a sharp drop (each ~0.005 lower than prior), but cumulative drift is significant; the team only notices when the SOC reports a missed-fraud spike.

An LLM support agent's RAG answer-grounding score was 0.88 at deployment; vendor upgraded the underlying model mid-month and grounding dropped to 0.74; no team PR was opened so no CI gate fired; customers start complaining about irrelevant citations.

solution.approach: Create a config file at `eval/drift/config.yaml` in the workload repository, declaring: (1) the per-dimension metrics to monitor (rubric-dimension scores for catalogue authoring; accuracy / precision / recall for an ML model; retrieval-hit-rate or answer-grounding for RAG; whatever the workload exposes); (2) per-dimension baseline values anchored to the workload's deployment (the model version and harness version the baseline is from); (3) per-dimension cadence (daily, weekly, per-event); (4) per-dimension alert thresholds (window-to-window drop; optionally a cumulative-from-baseline drop); (5) alert routing (channel and recipients). Build or adopt a scheduled monitor runtime that runs the declared metrics on cadence and logs per-dimension scores to a known location (`eval/drift/runs/`). Build or adopt an alerter runtime that watches those logs and dispatches alerts per the declared routing when thresholds are crossed. When the deployed model is updated (model version, prompt version, agent configuration that materially affects outputs), update the baseline reference in `eval/drift/config.yaml` OR commit an ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why the existing baseline still holds. The config file (the metrics list, baselines, cadences, thresholds, routing) is what Gate 1 inspects; the monitor and alerter runtimes are workload-level engineering choices this principle does not gate directly.
key_benefits: Catches the cumulative-drift failure mode that per-PR CI gates cannot — slow degradation in production outputs (or input distribution shifting away from harness coverage) that never crosses a per-PR threshold but compounds into significant regression over weeks or months. The first failure shape GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03 do not address.

Builds on GO1B1-01 / GO1B1-02 / GO1B1-03 without overlap. GO1B1-01 establishes the harness; GO1B1-02 stratifies it; GO1B1-03 encapsulates the metrics. GO1B1-04 runs those metrics on a cadence post-deployment against the baseline the harness produced. Each principle handles a distinct gate point; together they close the loop from authoring through production.

Baseline-update coupling (Gate 2) prevents the drift monitor itself from going silently stale. When the deployed model changes, the baseline reference must be refreshed or explicitly justified — no quiet drift from comparing today's model against last year's baseline.

Config-as-code for the monitor itself. Cadence, thresholds, baselines, routing all live in a versioned artefact reviewed in PRs and inspectable at audit, not in a vendor dashboard's UI or someone's head.

Workload-agnostic. The architectural rule (declare metrics + baseline + cadence + thresholds + routing; couple baseline updates to model changes) applies whether the workload is agent, RAG, LLM, or ML — only the specific metrics differ. The principle stays narrow; implementation flexes.

gates.check: On any PR, `eval/drift/config.yaml` must exist and declare: cadence (per-section or per-dimension); per-dimension thresholds (output metric thresholds + input distribution divergence thresholds); baseline reference (which model version and harness version the baseline is anchored to); alert routing (where alerts are sent). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that updates the deployed model (changes `model_version`, `prompt_version`, or any agent configuration that materially affects model outputs), `eval/drift/config.yaml` must update its baseline reference OR include a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why the existing baseline is still valid. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-04 — Monitor production inputs and model outputs for drift against the deployment baseline — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

The principle requires each deployed AI workload to operate a scheduled drift monitor. The monitor compares (a) current model evaluation metrics against the baseline established at deployment, and (b) the distribution of production inputs against the input distribution of the ground-truth harness. The monitor's metrics, baselines, cadences, thresholds, and alert routing live in a discoverable artefact (this catalogue's reference implementation: `eval/drift/config.yaml` in the workload repository). When the deployed model is updated, the baseline reference must be refreshed or explicitly justified via an ADR, so the monitor never compares today's model against a stale anchor.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a cumulative-drift failure that compounds slowly post-deployment, in the environment-shift sub-shape: a static system surrounded by an evolving world. The team has a workload in production with all three sibling disciplines in place — harness committed (GO1B1-01), stratified (GO1B1-02), with encapsulated metrics (GO1B1-03). Per-PR CI gates pass on every change, because no individual PR introduces a regression. The team is not careless; they are doing everything the first three principles ask. But over weeks or months, something drifts that is not in the team's control. Production input mix shifts as customer behaviour evolves. A foundation-model provider updates the underlying model parameters in a 'minor' release. A RAG index gets refreshed and the new corpus has different shape. An upstream data source's distribution changes. None of these trigger a PR in the team's repository, so per-PR gates never fire.

The drift in any single comparison window is small — sub-threshold, individually unremarkable. But it accumulates. By the time the failure surfaces, the workload has been silently underperforming for weeks or months, possibly through several quarters. The trigger is always external: a quarterly business review that segments outcomes by category and notices the shift; a customer escalation about a class of cases that has been quietly mis-handled; an internal audit that compares production decisions against the deployment-time baseline and finds them materially different; a regulator asking why the production approval rate looks different from what was filed at deployment. The cost is regulatory exposure, customer impact, manual remediation, and the loss of trust that comes from 'we did not know.'

The principle's value is forcing the team to declare, at deployment time, a scheduled comparison against the baseline with per-dimension thresholds and explicit alert routing. The drift becomes visible at the point it crosses the threshold, not at the point an external party finally notices.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-04 is mandatory for agent, RAG, and LLM-Only workloads (anywhere outputs or input distributions can drift over time after deployment). Nice-to-have for classical ML workloads, where established drift-monitoring tooling typically already exists. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting that GO1B1-04 is nice-to-have rather than mandatory for ML workloads because classical drift-monitoring tooling usually already covers the same ground; the example below is illustrative of how the failure would manifest if the team's ML workload were not covered by such tooling.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-04's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload in production at the time the baseline was established. Be specific about the industry and implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Crucially: establish the baseline. What were the model's metrics at deployment? What did the input distribution look like? What did 'good' look like? Make clear the team validated the workload before launch and has not made significant changes to it since — the workload is static and was working. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe how the workload drifts silently over a sustained period — weeks or months. Walk through chronologically: name the thing that drifts (production input mix, a foundation-model provider's minor release, RAG corpus refresh, upstream data source distribution change, customer behaviour evolution) and emphasise that this is OUTSIDE the team's control — no PR is opened. Make clear how small each per-window change is, and why per-PR CI gates do not catch it. Then describe the external trigger that surfaces the cumulative drift — quarterly business review, customer escalation, internal audit, regulator query — and how far past the baseline the workload had drifted by then. Name roles (head of category ops, audit team, duty compliance officer). Make the consequence concrete — regulatory exposure, customer disputes, remediation effort, time spent reconstructing what changed when. The failure must demonstrate something a scheduled drift monitor with per-dimension thresholds would have caught at the point of drift, not at the point of external discovery. NOT a generic 'the model got worse over time' regression — the specific shape is sub-threshold-per-window cumulative drift in a static system surrounded by an evolving environment."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 4% drift over six months', 'about three quarters before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

statement.title: Refresh the ground-truth harness on a declared cadence to reflect changes in production inputs and usage patterns
statement.description: The ground-truth evaluation harness established under GO1B1-01 must be refreshed on a declared cadence so it continues to represent the inputs and usage patterns the workload sees in production. Each refresh adds, updates, or retires scenarios. The cadence and any trigger conditions (calendar-based, drift-triggered, or both) are declared in a versioned artefact in the workload repository — the discipline is auditable, not ad-hoc.

problem.description: Without periodic refresh of the ground-truth harness, the harness drifts from current production reality. The original harness was captured against the inputs and usage patterns the workload saw at deployment; as production shifts — customer behaviour evolves, upstream data changes, edge cases appear — the harness still passes, but it now tests against a workload state that no longer exists. CI shows green; production still breaks. GO1B1-04's drift monitor may fire, but without a refresh discipline, every new PR continues to be gated against a stale baseline that misses the failure modes current production actually produces. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: Returns-triage agent's harness was captured against Q1 return reasons; by Q4, seasonal shift (holiday returns, winter clothing complaints, post-Christmas exchanges) makes up 40% of production volume — none of which is in the harness; CI still passes against Q1 patterns while production decisions on Q4 returns increasingly miss.

Retailer launches a new product category six months after the harness was committed; no scenarios for the new domain are added; CI passes every PR while the new category's returns hit HUMAN_REVIEW at three times the original baseline rate because the agent has no expected-decision anchor for it.

Customer vocabulary drifts (new slang, emoji-heavy reasons, regional dialect spread); harness scenarios use older phrasing; agent's interpretation accuracy slips on current inputs but the harness still scores high because the same older-phrased scenarios pass; team notices only when the escalation queue grows.

GO1B1-04's drift monitor alerts every quarter that production inputs have diverged from baseline; team logs the alert but no PR adds new scenarios; six months later the monitor is still firing against the same stale baseline; CI eval still green; production decisions increasingly out of sync.

solution.approach: Declare the refresh cadence and trigger conditions in a versioned artefact at a known path (convention: `eval/refresh/config.yaml`). The config names the calendar cadence (e.g. quarterly), any triggers from sibling principles (e.g. GO1B1-04 drift alert), and the named owner of each refresh cycle. Each refresh produces a PR that adds, updates, or retires scenarios in `eval/scenarios/` (the harness from GO1B1-01) and records the refresh metadata (date, trigger, scenario diff summary) in `eval/refresh/log.yaml`. A pre-merge CI gate checks two things: the refresh config exists, and the last refresh recorded in the log was within the declared cadence (or has a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` explaining the gap). A second gate fires on PRs touching `src/agent/` after a GO1B1-04 drift-monitor alert — those PRs must include a refresh entry or an ADR justifying why the alert does not warrant refresh. The Project Architect confirms each refresh cycle's scope at release as part of self-attestation.
key_benefits: Catches the stale-harness failure that per-PR CI gates and drift monitors cannot — drift detection without refresh discipline produces a permanently firing alert against a never-updated baseline; refresh closes the loop.

Couples to sibling principles: GO1B1-01 mandates the harness exists, GO1B1-02 stratifies it, GO1B1-03 encapsulates its metrics, GO1B1-04 detects when production drifts; GO1B1-05 refreshes the baseline so the loop converges rather than firing forever.
Refresh metadata in `eval/refresh/log.yaml` makes the cadence auditable — a reader can answer 'when was the harness last refreshed, what changed, and why' without archaeology through commit history.
Config-as-code for the refresh policy itself. Cadence, triggers, and named ownership all live in a versioned artefact reviewed in PRs and inspectable at audit, not in a wiki page or team memory.

Workload-agnostic at the discipline level — declare cadence, log refresh events, gate against stale baselines applies whether the workload is agent, RAG, LLM, or ML; only the artefact being refreshed differs.

gates.check: On any PR, `eval/refresh/config.yaml` must exist and declare: calendar cadence; trigger conditions from sibling principles (e.g. GO1B1-04 drift alerts); named owner per refresh cycle. The most recent refresh recorded in `eval/refresh/log.yaml` must be within the declared cadence, OR a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` must justify the gap. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR touching files under `src/agent/` after a GO1B1-04 drift-monitor alert fired since the last commit on the integration branch, the PR must include a refresh entry in `eval/refresh/log.yaml` (with corresponding scenario diffs in `eval/scenarios/`) OR a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why the alert does not warrant refresh. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Each refresh adds, updates, or retires scenarios so the harness continues to represent the inputs and usage patterns the workload sees in production. CI gates enforce both: the cadence config exists and the last refresh was within the declared interval; and any PR touching agent code after a drift-monitor alert (GO1B1-04) must include a refresh entry or a linked ADR justifying why the alert does not warrant refresh.

The team has a workload in production with all four sibling disciplines in place — harness committed (GO1B1-01), stratified (GO1B1-02), with encapsulated metrics (GO1B1-03), and a drift monitor firing (GO1B1-04). YOUR TASK

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{

Make clear the team is competent and doing everything the first four principles ask. Do NOT describe the failure here.",

The failure must demonstrate something a refresh cadence with audit log would have caught at the point of drift, not a generic 'the model got worse over time'."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The returns triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 40% of Q4 volume', 'about three quarters before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only.
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

statement.title: Pin every model call to an immutable, catalogued version and re-run the evaluation harness before any version change ships
statement.description: Every model reference in a workload must resolve to an exact, immutable model version drawn from an approved model catalog — never a floating alias (e.g. a `-latest` pointer) and never a runtime-resolved default. A pre-merge gate fails any model reference that is not a catalogued pinned version; and any PR that changes a pinned model identifier must re-run the workload's evaluation harness (GO1B1-01) and clear its baseline bar before it can merge. This makes a model change impossible without a reviewed PR and impossible to ship without re-evaluation — so the foundation model cannot silently move under the agent via provider roll-forwards, deprecations, or gateway default upgrades.

problem.description: Without pinning model references to immutable versions and gating model changes on re-evaluation, the foundation model can change underneath a workload without anyone deciding it should. A team that calls the model through a floating alias, a provider `-latest` pointer, or a shared-gateway default inherits whatever the provider or platform ships next — a roll-forward, a deprecation-and-migration, a quietly upgraded default. The agent's own code, prompts, and tests are untouched, so no PR is opened and no per-PR or drift gate fires; infrastructure dashboards stay green because the model is responding, just differently. The behaviour shifts — tone, verbosity, formatting, refusal rate, a few-shot pattern the prompt relied on — and the first signal is customers, not CI. This is distinct from the gradual cumulative drift GO1B1-04 watches for: a model swap is a discrete step-change at a single moment, which a window-to-window drift threshold can miss entirely and which has no per-PR trigger at all. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A retail customer-support chatbot calls the model via the provider's `-latest` alias; overnight the provider rolls the alias to a newer, more cautious model; by morning the bot hedges every answer with disclaimers and refuses routine account questions it used to handle; no PR was opened, infra dashboards are green, and the team only learns of it from a CSAT drop a week later.

A workload pins the exact model in dev but reads the model name from an env var in prod that defaults to the gateway's current default; a platform-wide gateway upgrade swaps that default; the prod agent silently runs a different model than dev, and the divergence is invisible until output differences are reported by a downstream team.

A provider deprecates the dated model snapshot a workload used and auto-migrates its calls to the successor; response formatting changes just enough to break a downstream JSON parser the agent's outputs feed; the breakage is attributed to 'the model getting worse' rather than to an un-pinned version that moved.

A team upgrades the model deliberately by editing one line, assuming the newer model is strictly better; it regresses on a minority class of cases the eval harness already covers, but nothing forces the harness to re-run on a model change, so the regression ships and surfaces as customer complaints weeks later.

A model swap lands a clean overnight step-change in behaviour that falls inside a single drift-monitor window, so GO1B1-04's window-to-window threshold never flags it; with no gate on the model identifier itself, there is no control positioned to catch the change at its source.

solution.approach: Pin every model reference to an exact, immutable version drawn from an approved model catalog, and gate model changes on re-evaluation. (1) Maintain a model catalog of approved pinned model identifiers (convention: `models/catalog.yaml`, platform-owned), each entry an exact version — a dated provider snapshot or a self-hosted weights digest — never an alias. (2) A pre-merge lint scans the workload for every model reference (config, code, env defaults) and fails the PR unless each resolves to a catalog entry; floating aliases (`-latest`, `default`) and un-catalogued identifiers fail. (3) The central model client / SDK refuses at runtime to call any model not resolved to a catalog entry, closing the dynamically-resolved-alias hole the static lint cannot see — the same contract shape as GO3B1-01's SDK refusing inline prompts, applied to model identifiers. (4) A pre-merge gate detects any change to a pinned model identifier (diff vs the merge base) and requires the workload's evaluation harness (GO1B1-01) to re-run and clear its baseline bar before the PR can merge; a model change that fails the harness blocks. (5) Because the version is pinned in config, rollback after a bad model is a one-line revert to the prior catalog entry. The model catalog, the catalog-membership lint, and the SDK refusal are platform-team-owned; the workload owns which catalogued version it pins and the eval result on change. This principle gates the model-change trigger; it reuses GO1B1-01's harness for the evaluation itself and does not re-implement evaluation machinery.
key_benefits: Makes a model change impossible without a reviewed PR and impossible to ship without re-evaluation — the silent overnight provider roll-forward that today surfaces as a CSAT drop becomes a blocked, evaluated, deliberate change.

Closes the hole the drift monitor can miss — a model swap is a discrete step-change, not the gradual sub-threshold drift GO1B1-04 watches for; this gate catches the change at its source (the model identifier) rather than after the behaviour has already shifted in production.

Catalog membership enforces pinning by construction — the catalog holds only exact versions, so 'must be in the catalog' means 'must be pinned'; the runtime SDK refusal extends the guarantee to env-default and dynamically-resolved references the static lint cannot see (the GO3B1-01 SDK pattern, applied to models).

Re-evaluation reuses the existing eval harness — no new evaluation machinery; the model-change gate simply re-runs GO1B1-01 against the new version and gates on the baseline, turning 'we assumed the new model was better' into 'the new model passed our bar'.
Rollback is a one-line config revert — because the version is pinned, recovering from a bad model is reverting one catalog reference, not a scramble to reconstruct what changed and when.

gates.check: On any PR, a lint scans the workload for every foundation-model reference (model identifiers in config such as `eval/config.yaml` or `models/*.yaml`, in `src/`, and in environment-variable defaults) and fails the PR unless each reference resolves to an entry in the approved model catalog (`models/catalog.yaml` or the platform-owned catalog it points to). Floating aliases (`-latest`, `default`, an undated provider pointer) and identifiers absent from the catalog fail, since every catalog entry is an exact immutable version (a dated provider snapshot or a self-hosted weights digest). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that changes a pinned model identifier (diff vs the merge base on any model reference the catalog-membership lint tracks), the workload's evaluation harness (GO1B1-01) must re-run against the new model version and clear its declared baseline bar; the PR fails if the harness has not re-run for the new identifier, or if it re-ran and did not pass the baseline, unless a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifies the exception. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.
