explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-06 — Pin every model call to an immutable, catalogued version and re-run the evaluation harness before any version change ships — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every model reference in a workload must resolve to an exact, immutable model version drawn from an approved model catalog — never a floating alias (a `-latest` pointer) and never a runtime-resolved default. A pre-merge lint fails any model reference that is not a catalogued pinned version; the central model SDK refuses at runtime to call an un-pinned model; and any change to a pinned model identifier must re-run the workload's evaluation harness and clear its baseline bar before it can merge. Because the version is pinned, rollback is a one-line config revert.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure: the pin-and-re-evaluate discipline was never built, and a discrete model-substitution event makes the absence visible. The team has a workload in production that calls the foundation model — but through a floating alias, a provider `-latest` pointer, or a shared-gateway default rather than a pinned, catalogued version. Nothing watches the model identity, and no gate couples a model change to re-evaluation. Locally everything looks fine: the model responds, latency and error-rate dashboards are green, and the team has not changed their own code. The discipline that nobody set up is the one that makes a model change a deliberate, reviewed, evaluated event instead of something that can happen TO the workload from outside.

The absence becomes visible at a discrete triggering moment that is outside the team's control: the provider rolls the `-latest` alias forward to a newer model overnight; the provider deprecates the dated snapshot the workload used and auto-migrates its calls to a successor; a platform-wide gateway upgrade changes the default model the workload's env var resolves to. The agent's code, prompts, and tests are untouched, so no PR is opened and no per-PR or drift gate fires. But the model underneath has changed, and its behaviour shifts — more verbose, more cautious, different formatting, a higher refusal rate, a few-shot pattern the prompt relied on no longer honoured. The first signal is not CI; it is customers — a CSAT drop, a spike in complaints, a downstream parser breaking on the new output format, a class of requests quietly mishandled. The team cannot point to a change they made, because they did not make one; the change happened to them, and there was no control positioned to catch a foundation model moving underneath a workload that was otherwise standing still.

The principle's value is forcing the model to be a pinned, catalogued artefact that cannot move without a reviewed PR, and cannot ship without re-evaluation — so a model change is always deliberate, always evaluated, and always one revert away from rollback.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-06 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads (anywhere a foundation model is called and can be moved by a provider, a deprecation, or a gateway). It is nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads, which typically already carry model-version discipline through established MLOps registries. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for foundation-model workloads where the model is a provider-served dependency that can change outside the team's control, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest if an ML workload's model registry did not pin and re-validate on version change.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-06's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with a workload in production that calls a foundation model. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and how the model is referenced — crucially, an UN-pinned way: a provider `-latest` alias, an env var defaulting to a shared gateway's current model, a model name copied between repos. Hint offhandedly that nobody watches model identity, that the budget/quality was validated once at launch, that model upgrades have never been a review-time concern. The reader should picture a confident team that does not realise the model is a moving dependency. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the model moves underneath the workload. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (the provider rolls the `-latest` alias forward; the provider deprecates the dated snapshot and auto-migrates; a gateway upgrade swaps the default). Emphasise that this is OUTSIDE the team's control and that NO PR is opened, so per-PR and drift gates never fire and infra dashboards stay green. Then describe how the behaviour shifts (verbosity, caution, formatting, refusal rate, a broken downstream parser) and how the team discovers it — a CSAT drop, customer complaints, a downstream team reporting output differences — not from CI. Name roles (the platform engineer who eventually correlates it to the model change; the support lead escalating; the on-call who finds the version moved days ago). Make the consequence concrete — degraded customer experience across every interaction for days, a scramble to identify what changed, a rollback that is hard because nothing was pinned. The failure must demonstrate something a pinned, catalogued model plus a re-eval-on-change gate would have caught at the moment of change — not a generic 'the model got worse' regression, and specifically NOT gradual drift (this is a discrete swap)."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage chatbot' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly a week before anyone correlated it', 'about a 20% jump in refusals').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly service review', 'the platform team's incident review').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

references.question: GENOPS03 — How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?

implementation_step.title: Set up Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Set up Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create the initial prompt templates by developing a foundational set of prompt templates tailored to your use case. Incorporate variables within prompts to enhance flexibility and adaptability. Implement a robust versioning system to track changes and iterations of prompt templates.

references.note: AWS step 1 mandates a versioned prompt template management system with variables, expressed in vendor-specific terms (Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management). This principle absorbs the architectural mandate — every prompt is a registered, versioned, addressable template with declared variables — and adds four concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the SDK call-signature contract `(template_id, variables)` refusing inline strings as runtime enforcement; (2) the manifest schema declaring per-template metadata (id, version, body, variables, output_schema, model, runtime_token_budget, owner, status); (3) two-mode storage neutrality (file-based + manifest vs hosted TMS) with mode-parameterised CI lints; (4) the no-inline-call AST lint banning direct provider-SDK invocations from workload code. AWS step 4 (Integrate prompts into applications — use Bedrock SDK during inference) is absorbed into this principle: the SDK call-signature contract is inseparable from the template registry. Steps 2 / 3 / 5 / 6 are non-promoted — step 2 (baseline performance eval) and step 3 (A/B variant testing) absorbed by the GO1B1 eval-harness family; step 5 (periodic review) is process advice without a commitable artefact; step 6 (cross-team collaboration) is the mechanical consequence of step 1's central registry.

IV.A's expectation that governance policies be operationalised through standards and processes is directly served by the manifest schema and the SDK enforcement. statement.title: Route every model call through a registered, versioned prompt template via the central SDK
statement.description: Every prompt used in production lives as a registered, versioned template in the workload repository, addressable by `template_id`. The central LLM SDK refuses inline-string prompts and accepts only `(template_id, variables)` call signatures, making the template registry the single source of truth for prompt content and the substrate every downstream cost / quality / safety gate consumes.

problem.description: Without registered, versioned prompt templates accessed via a central SDK that refuses inline strings, prompt content lives as f-strings scattered across the codebase — invisible as discrete artefacts, untrackable by template identity, and unauditable as anything other than git archaeology over the assembled runtime view. The failure compounds because the path of least resistance for any deadline-pressured engineer is the inline call, and once even one workload bypasses the registry, the SDK's contract becomes voluntary across the portfolio rather than mandatory. Every downstream gate that would key on template identity — token budgets, PII content review, safety classification, model-routing policies, prompt-drift monitoring — collapses, because there is no addressable thing for the gate to attach to. Versioning becomes git-history reconstruction across unrelated commits rather than a first-class field, making rollback after a bad model upgrade or prompt change a manual reconstruction exercise. The central platform team cannot enforce the contract retroactively because the entry point that should refuse non-registered calls has already been permissive — within a release cycle every team builds its own pattern and drift sets in. (Examples below are illustrative narratives, not documented incidents.)
examples: A classifier prompt is assembled at runtime from three f-string fragments concatenated across `src/agents/triage.py`, `src/agents/prompts.py`, and `src/utils/policy_text.py`; when a compliance reviewer asks for the exact prompt currently routed to the model, an engineer has to run the assembly in a debugger and paste the result, with no guarantee the captured form matches the next invocation.

A workload keeps prompt bodies under `prompts/` in the repo but the central SDK still accepts raw strings; a deadline-pressured engineer inlines a new endpoint's prompt directly into the handler, the registry silently goes out of date, and the discrepancy is invisible until a portfolio-wide audit months later flags untracked template-shaped content in `src/`.

Six months after the catalogue ships a token-budget principle keyed on `template_id`, a workload still using inline-string prompts cannot register any budgets — the budget gate has no addressable thing to bite on — and the principle is unenforceable on that workload until a multi-week prompt-extraction project is funded.

A model upgrade requires rewriting every prompt for the new context-window shape; with no version field on the prompts, the rollback path after a regression is `git revert` across 14 unrelated commits, and the previous prompt content is reconstructed from memory by the engineer who originally wrote it, shipping the rollback with subtle drift.

A regulator asks for the exact prompt and variable schema in production for the customer-facing summarisation endpoint as of a specific date; with no manifest, no version field, and prompt fragments scattered across handlers, the answer is reconstructed by grepping git history and reading multiple file diffs against the requested date — a several-hour archaeology exercise that becomes a recurring tax across audits.

solution.approach: All AI workload prompts flow through a single enterprise SDK with a `(template_id, variables)` call signature; the SDK refuses inline-string prompts at runtime and rejects any call referencing a `template_id` not present in the workload's registry. Every registered template carries a stable metadata schema: `id`, `version`, body content, declared `variables`, `output_schema` (when applicable), `model`, `runtime_token_budget` (input + output), `owner`, and `status` (draft / active / deprecated / archived). The principle is neutral on storage backend; the workload picks one of two registration modes, both producing the same SDK-visible contract. **Mode A (file-based + manifest):** bodies at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}`, metadata rows in `prompts/manifest.yaml`; git is the version backend; CI lints walk `prompts/` and `src/` for template_id ↔ manifest row ↔ body file consistency. Fit for single-repo workloads with engineering-owned prompt content. **Mode B (hosted TMS):** bodies and metadata in a hosted prompt management service; the SDK fetches at build time (cached) or at runtime; CI lints query the service API for the same consistency checks. Specific vendor services and their adapter implementations are listed in `framework_mappings` notes. Fit for multi-repo enterprises sharing templates cross-workload, or for workloads with business / product stakeholders editing prompt content through a UI. Project teams in either mode call `sdk.generate(template_id="X", variables={...})`; the SDK resolves the template_id from the configured backend, composes system + user content, validates supplied variables against the declared schema, tokenises the assembled prompt and the response using the model's own tokenizer to log actual-vs-declared budget, and dispatches the API call with `max_tokens` set to the declared output budget. Direct calls to provider SDKs (`anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create`, `openai.OpenAI().responses.create`, the Bedrock / Azure equivalents) from workload code are prohibited; providers are reached only via the central SDK's adapter layer. The central platform team owns the SDK, the registration metadata schema, the CI lint suite (parameterised per mode), the tokenizer integration, and the TMS-adapter set if Mode B is supported organisation-wide; project teams own the prompt body content, the metadata field values, and the budget rationale in their change history. An escape-hatch `dev_only=True` flag permits unregistered template calls against dev model endpoints only, so prototype iteration does not require a registration round-trip.
key_benefits: Prompt content becomes an addressable artefact — `template_id` keys everything downstream (cost dashboards, content review, safety classification, drift monitoring); the substrate the entire prompt-governance stack depends on exists by mandate, not by accident.

Versioning is first-class — the registry's `version` field plus the body's content-addressed history make rollback a one-row revert rather than a 14-commit reconstruction; regulator answers come from one registry query, not from grep + reasoning over assembled f-strings.

Token budgets become enforceable — the registry declares `input_token_budget` and `output_token_budget` per template; the SDK measures actual on every call via the model's own tokenizer and emits overrun signals; the downstream GENCOST03-BP01 budget principle has an addressable thing to bite on.

Inline-string bypass is mechanically impossible — the SDK refuses raw-string calls at runtime, the CI lint blocks direct provider-SDK imports from workload code, and the no-orphan-template lint catches drift between registry and body content; the contract is enforced at three layers rather than relying on developer discipline.

Ownership and lifecycle are explicit — every template has a named `owner` (CODEOWNERS-style routing for reviews and incidents) and a `status` field that lets dev and prod environments diverge cleanly (draft templates rejected in prod; deprecated templates log warnings and feed a migration queue).

gates.check: Lint walks `src/` and extracts every `template_id` referenced in calls to the central LLM SDK (AST scan for `sdk.generate(template_id=...)`, `sdk.call(template_id=...)`, and equivalent entry points). Each extracted `template_id` must resolve to a registry entry: Mode A workloads require both a row in `prompts/manifest.yaml` AND a matching body file at the declared `body` path; Mode B workloads require the configured TMS API to return a record for that id. Conversely, every body file under `prompts/` (Mode A) or every record in the workload's TMS inventory (Mode B) must have a matching reference in `src/`, and every registry row must declare non-empty values for `id`, `version`, `body`, `variables`, `model`, `runtime_token_budget.input`, `runtime_token_budget.output`, `owner`, and `status` ∈ {draft, active, deprecated, archived}. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

AST-grep lint scans `src/` for direct invocations of provider model SDKs: `anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create()`, `openai.OpenAI().responses.create()` and `openai.OpenAI().chat.completions.create()`, `boto3.client("bedrock-runtime").invoke_model()`, Azure OpenAI client `.chat.completions.create()` and `.responses.create()`, plus any others on the prohibited-imports list at `prompts/prohibited_calls.yaml`. The list is maintained by the platform team and pinned with the central LLM SDK version. Any direct invocation found in workload code outside the central LLM SDK package fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central LLM SDK at a version ≥ the floor declared in `prompts/sdk_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each registry-schema cutover (new required metadata fields, breaking changes to the SDK call signature, deprecated tokenizer adapter). Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated registration schemas or call signatures from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO3B1-01 — Route every model call through a registered, versioned prompt template via the central SDK — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every prompt used in production lives as a registered, versioned template addressable by `template_id`, with its body, declared variables, output schema, model, token budgets, owner, and lifecycle status declared in a workload registry (file-based + manifest, or a hosted prompt management service). The central LLM SDK refuses inline-string prompts at runtime and accepts only `(template_id, variables)` call signatures; direct invocations of provider model SDKs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Bedrock, Azure) from workload code are prohibited. A central platform team owns the SDK, the registration metadata schema, the CI lint suite (template_id consistency, no-inline-call ban, version floor), and the tokenizer integration; project teams own the prompt body content and the metadata field values.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible the moment the enterprise needs to answer a portfolio-level question about its prompts, or the moment a downstream gate (token budgets, safety classification, prompt-drift monitoring) tries to operate on a `template_id` that doesn't exist. Multiple project teams have shipped AI workloads, each writing its prompts inline as Python f-strings, concatenated YAML, or string templates scattered across handlers and utility modules. Each team's setup looks fine locally: the prompts work, the model returns sensible output, the on-call engineer can find the file. The discipline that nobody built is the central one: there is no template registry, no SDK contract refusing raw strings, no versioning of prompts as discrete artefacts, no addressable `template_id` for downstream tooling to key on.

The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: finance commissions a portfolio-wide prompt-cost dashboard and discovers cost attribution cannot be done without manually identifying every prompt in every workload; a model upgrade requires rewriting prompts and the rollback path after a regression turns into a multi-day git-archaeology exercise per workload; the catalogue ships a token-budget principle keyed on `template_id` and workloads with inline prompts cannot register any budgets because there is no addressable thing to bind a budget to; a regulator asks for the exact prompt and variable schema that produced a specific customer-facing decision and the answer requires running the assembly in a debugger and pasting the result, with no guarantee the captured form matches what the next invocation would produce.

At that moment the enterprise discovers that the cost of retrofitting prompt registration — extracting inline strings into template files, declaring variables, refactoring every call site to use the SDK, building the lint suite, training every team on the contract — is many times the cost of having mandated the SDK contract before the second workload shipped. The principle's value is forcing the registry and the SDK contract to exist before the second workload is built, so prompt cost attribution, prompt versioning, prompt content review, downstream safety classification, and audit-time reconstruction become emergent properties of the system rather than retrofits paid for in audit findings, finance reconciliation hours, and multi-quarter remediation projects.

APPLICABILITY

GO3B1-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads — anywhere prompts exist as a first-class artefact mediating model behaviour. It is nice-to-have for traditional ML workloads, which typically have no prompt-shaped artefacts beyond model cards or feature descriptions. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open `use_case_background` with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where prompts ARE the primary artefact, and the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest inside an ML pipeline whose closest analogue is unmanaged feature-engineering text or experiment configuration.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO3B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise that has multiple AI workloads in production. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three workloads owned by different teams, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and how each team's prompts are currently stored (inline f-strings in handlers, ad-hoc YAML files without registration, copy-paste between repos). The reader should picture a confident enterprise where each individual team's prompts work fine — the model responds, the feature ships, the team's on-call can debug. Crucially: hint at the absence offhandedly — note that field names for prompts differ across teams, that there is no central catalogue of what prompts are in production, that prompt versioning relies on git history of the file that happened to contain the string. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the absent registry becomes visible. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the triggering event (finance asks for portfolio-level prompt-cost attribution; a model upgrade forces a prompt rewrite and rollback fails; a token-budget gate is mandated by the catalogue but cannot bite because there are no template_ids; a regulator asks for the exact prompt in production as of a specific date). Then describe what the enterprise tries to do and where it falls apart — emailing teams, grepping repositories, reconstructing assembled prompts in debuggers, reasoning over git diffs to identify the prompt that was live on the requested date. Name roles (the platform engineer trying to build a prompt-cost dashboard; the SRE on-call trying to rollback a model upgrade; the compliance officer fielding a regulator's prompt-content request). Make the consequence concrete — measurable hours lost per audit request, a budget principle that cannot be applied to half the portfolio, a regulator commitment that cannot be met from the current evidence, a retrofit project that is now multi-quarter. The failure must demonstrate something a central SDK + registered template registry + version field would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'we don't document our prompts well' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The triage classifier prompt in src/agents/triage.py' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('about six teams', 'roughly two weeks per audit request').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

references.question: GENOPS03 — How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?
references.best_practice: GENOPS03-BP02 — Enable tracing for agents and RAG workflows

implementation_step.title: Collect and aggregate trace data
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Implement a system to collect trace data from agents involved in your parallel processing workflow. After running an Amazon Bedrock Agent, view the trace in real-time as your agent performs orchestration. When making an InvokeAgent request to the Amazon Bedrock runtime endpoint, set the enableTrace field to TRUE. This will include a trace field in the InvokeAgent response for each chunk in the stream. Store this data in a centralized location, such as Amazon S3 or Amazon CloudWatch Logs, for quick access and analysis.

references.note: AWS step 1 calls for centralised collection and aggregation of agent trace data but expresses the mandate in vendor-specific terms (Bedrock InvokeAgent enableTrace flag, S3 or CloudWatch Logs as the persistence target). This principle absorbs the architectural mandate — every model call, tool invocation, and retrieval query emits a correlated span under a shared trace ID, persisted to a centrally-managed observability backend — and adds three concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the canonical event schema and header/payload split that makes traces from different teams comparable, (2) the central SDK and adapter pattern that makes the backend swap-able rather than locked to one AWS service, and (3) the merge-gate enforcement that prevents direct backend calls. AWS step 2 (Secure trace data — PII access control) and the implicit cost-attribution use case are also absorbed; step 1 is the closest AWS-verbatim anchor for the emission mandate.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.B: Implement Ongoing Risk Assessments and Monitoring
references.note: Primary mapping. Centralised trace emission with mandatory header fields (project_id, workload_pattern, model_id, compliance_tier) is the technical substrate that makes ongoing monitoring possible at portfolio scale — without it, IV.B's expected risk-assessment cadence cannot be operated because evidence is fragmented across team-specific backends. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP IV.B competency definition to promote to verified.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.C: Establish Security, Access Control, and Incident Management
references.note: Secondary mapping. The central PII pre-scrub, the approved-backends allow-list (gate 1), and trace-correlation across team boundaries directly serve IV.C's access-control and incident-management scope — incidents that cross team boundaries become reconstructable from a single trace_id instead of timestamp-guessed across fragmented logs. Needs review against AIGP IV.C to verify.

statement.title: Centralised Observability SDK for AI Workloads
statement.description: All AI workload observability emission — every model call, tool invocation, and retrieval query — must be routed through the enterprise observability SDK. Each call carries a central-owned header (project_id, workload_pattern, target_backend, plus central enrichment) and a project-owned payload. Direct calls from workload code to any observability backend are prohibited; backends are reached only via the SDK's adapter layer.

problem.description: Generative AI workloads emit observability data — traces, metrics, logs — that the enterprise needs for cost attribution, incident reconstruction, audit evidence, and cross-team correlation. When each project team picks its own backend, writes its own emit code, and self-defines field names, the resulting fragmentation makes portfolio-level questions unanswerable. Cost attribution requires reconciling four price schemes by hand. PII leakage becomes per-team risk rather than a single chokepoint. Incidents that cross team boundaries are reconstructed by timestamp guessing instead of trace IDs. Schema drift between projects silently kills any chance of a cross-project dashboard. The failure modes only surface at multi-project scale, and by then the retrofit cost is large.
examples: Fragmented backends with no shared billing — Project A ships to Datadog, Project B to Langfuse, Project C self-hosts ClickHouse. Finance gets three invoices in three currencies with no per-project breakdown; allocating GenAI observability spend to teams takes a 2-week manual exercise each quarter.

PII leaks into logs because every team rolls its own redactor — a RAG project logs raw retrieved chunks to CloudWatch; one chunk contained an unredacted SSN from a support ticket. Discovered three months later in a SOC2 audit. No central redactor existed to catch it before emit.

Recurring cost queries that never converge — Finance asks 'what did GenAI cost us last month?'. Answering requires emailing six teams, reconciling four token-pricing schemes (prompt caching, batch, on-demand, fine-tuned), and accepting ±30% confidence. The number changes 15% each time it's recomputed because nobody owns the canonical answer.

Trace IDs do not propagate across team boundaries — a customer reports an agent gave wrong info; the agent called a retrieval service owned by a different team; the two systems use different correlation IDs. Reconstruction relies on timestamp guessing. The incident takes 4 hours instead of 15 minutes.

Schema drift makes cross-project dashboards impossible — Project A logs `tokens_in`, Project B logs `prompt_tokens`, Project C logs `input_token_count`. Building a portfolio cost dashboard needs bespoke ETL per project; no team owns it; it never gets built.

solution.approach: All AI workload observability emission flows through a single enterprise SDK. The event is split into a header (central-owned, schema-controlled) and a payload (project-owned, free-form). Project teams call `sdk.emit(project_id, workload_pattern, target_backend, payload)`. The SDK validates the call, enriches the header (cost_center, compliance_tier, sdk_version, dollar_cost computed from a central price table, trace correlation IDs, PII pre-scrub), maps the canonical event to the requested backend's schema (Langsmith runs, Datadog LLM Observability spans, etc.), and emits asynchronously. Direct calls to any observability backend from workload code are prohibited. An escape-hatch `custom_payload` field accepts opaque blobs the team owns end-to-end, so workload-specific shapes do not queue behind central schema changes.
key_benefits: Cross-team trace correlation works — one `trace_id` survives a request that crosses three teams' workloads, so multi-team incidents become reconstructable instead of timestamp-guessed.
Cost attribution is canonical — finance answers 'what did GenAI cost us?' from one price table applied uniformly, with per-project breakdown produced automatically rather than reconciled quarterly.
PII leakage gets caught at one chokepoint — the central pre-scrub runs before any backend emit, regardless of team or backend choice.
Backend swaps become a single SDK release — adding Langfuse or migrating off Datadog does not require N parallel team migrations.
Schema drift is eliminated — `prompt_tokens` means the same thing in every project's traces, so portfolio-level dashboards are buildable.

gates.check: Lint scans the workload codebase for direct calls to observability backends (Datadog `ddtrace` / `datadog.api`, `langsmith.Client`, `langfuse.Langfuse`, AWS `boto3.client('logs')`, raw HTTPS POSTs to observability ingest endpoints) and fails the PR if any are found outside the central SDK's adapter layer. The allow-list of permitted import paths is read from `observability/allowed_imports.yaml`, maintained by the platform team and pinned with the SDK version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

Lint inspects every call to `observability_sdk.emit()` and verifies the required header parameters are supplied with non-empty values: `project_id` (matches the project registry), `workload_pattern` ∈ {`rag`, `llm`, `agent`}, `target_backend` ∈ the SDK's supported-backends enum. Fails on any call missing a required parameter, supplying an unrecognised `workload_pattern`, or naming a `target_backend` not on the supported list. The mandatory-fields contract is read from the SDK package's pinned schema version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central observability SDK at a version ≥ the current floor declared in `observability/sdk_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each schema deprecation cutover. Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated schema versions from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

implementation_step.title: Secure trace data
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Secure trace data. Implement appropriate access controls to verify that only authorized personnel can view trace data. Be mindful of any sensitive information that might be included in traces and handle it according to your organization's security policies.

references.note: AWS step 2 mandates access controls so only authorised personnel can read trace data and that sensitive information in traces be handled per the organisation's security policies. The emission-time slice of this step (PII pre-scrub before any backend write) is absorbed by sibling principle GO3B2-01. This principle concretises the remaining post-emission slice — read-side access governance and retention — and adds three concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the compliance_tier classification flowing from emit (set in GO3B2-01) to read (this principle) as the keying field that ties the two halves of the audit trail together, (2) the central access policy file + retention policy file as the single source of truth backend IAM bindings and lifecycle rules are derived from, (3) the pre-merge IaC lint that blocks project-local IAM grants on observability backends, preventing the most common bypass. AWS itself nests this step within GENOPS03-BP02 (Traceability) rather than within GENSEC; the placement here matches the AWS source. Option B (defer to a future GENSEC walk under P22 Data Protection or P21 IAM) was considered and rejected on the audit-trail argument: compliance_tier flows from emit to read across the GO3B2 pair, and splitting governance across pillars would fragment the regulator-facing walk.

statement.title: Govern read access and retention on AI observability traces through a centrally-owned policy
statement.description: All AI observability traces emitted via the central SDK (sibling principle GO3B2-01) are governed at read time by a centrally-owned access policy keyed on the trace's compliance_tier, declared as code in the platform repository. Retention windows per compliance_tier are likewise declared centrally and enforced through backend lifecycle rules. Project workload IaC may not grant read access on observability backend resources directly — those grants flow exclusively from the central policy. Every read of regulated- or sensitive-tier traces is captured in a read-side audit log made available to the security team.

problem.description: AI observability traces stored across multiple backends contain residual sensitive metadata, business-relevant payload contents, and compliance_tier-classified data — none of which should be readable by every engineer with platform access. When read-side access controls are set up per project (team-local IAM grants on each backend) and retention is decided ad-hoc by whoever owns the backend, the post-emission half of the audit trail falls apart. A regulator asking 'show me who accessed PII-tagged trace X over the last 90 days, and prove the trace was deleted within the retention window' walks an evidence path that crosses N team boundaries with no central record. Per-project access controls drift silently: contractors lose access on one backend but retain it on another; former employees' role bindings outlast their employment; cross-team incident reconstructions stall on backend-by-backend access tickets. Retention windows that should be 30 days for sensitive traces end up at 13 months (vendor default) or indefinite (no lifecycle rule) because nobody owns the lifecycle. The failure surfaces at audit time, when retrofit cost is the highest.
examples: Contractor offboarded from project A; team A's IAM grant revoked, but team B's separate Langsmith read role for cross-team incident support is never touched; the contractor's existing token fetches sensitive traces for 6 weeks before a SOC2 spot-check catches the second grant.

Retention drift across backends — project A keeps Datadog spans at 30 days (vendor default), project B keeps Langsmith runs at 13 months (vendor default), project C never set a CloudWatch log-group retention rule so traces accumulate indefinitely; a GDPR DSAR for a customer whose production data was deleted 2 years ago turns up 4-year-old trace references in project C.

Cross-team incident reconstruction blocked at backend boundary — SRE investigating a cascading agent failure has read on team X's traces (Datadog) but not team Y's (Langfuse); the IAM grant ticket queues through team Y's manual approval for 3 days; by the time it lands, the live trace data in team X has rolled past the 30-day retention window so end-to-end correlation is no longer possible.

Read-side audit absent — an external auditor asks 'list everyone who read regulated-tier traces in Q1'; each project team's backend defaulted access logging to off; the answer is 'we don't know, and we can't reconstruct it.' Cited as a SOC2 deficiency.

compliance_tier honoured at emit, ignored at storage — GO3B2-01 stamps compliance_tier=regulated on a healthcare workload's traces, but the backend bucket has a single retention policy covering all tiers at 13 months, well beyond the 30-day cap the workload's compliance team had committed to in the SOC2 narrative; discovered during the SOC2 audit, requiring narrative remediation.

solution.approach: A central access policy file (`observability/access_policy.yaml`) declares, per compliance_tier and per project, the principals (roles, groups, contractors) authorised to read traces, with optional region scoping. A central retention policy file (`observability/retention_policy.yaml`) maps each compliance_tier to a maximum retention window, and central IaC owned by the platform team translates the policy into backend-specific lifecycle rules (S3 lifecycle, Datadog retention API, Langsmith TTL, CloudWatch log-group retention). Project workloads declare their `compliance_tier` in the existing `observability/config.yaml` (introduced by sibling principle GO3B2-01) and may not grant IAM read on observability backend resources directly — a pre-merge IaC lint blocks any project-local grant against the central observability ARN allow-list. Read-side audit logs from every backend feed a central security team queue; access requests for new backends or compliance_tier reclassifications go through a documented central security review with a 1-week SLA.
key_benefits: Audit trail is centrally walkable — 'who accessed trace X under what compliance_tier and when' answers from a single query against the central policy plus the read-audit log, instead of timestamp-reconciling across N team backends.

Retention drift is impossible by construction — compliance_tier is set at emit (GO3B2-01); the central retention policy translates that tier into backend lifecycle rules automatically; project teams cannot accidentally over-retain because they don't own the rules.
Contractor or role offboarding propagates from one place — removing a principal from the central policy file re-syncs every backend; no per-team revocation tickets, no orphan grants.

Cross-team incident reconstruction works because the access policy expresses incident-response roles centrally — SRE on-call gets read scoped to an active incident's `trace_id` family across all backends, no per-team IAM friction.
Read-side governance reuses the compliance_tier that already flows from emit (GO3B2-01) — no duplicated classification, no fragmenting of the post-emission audit trail across pillars or focus areas.

gates.check: On any PR, `observability/config.yaml` at the workload repo root must declare `compliance_tier` ∈ {regulated, sensitive, standard}, AND the declared value must match the workload's compliance_tier in the central project registry. Pre-merge lint fails on missing field, unrecognised value, or mismatch against the registry. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, lint scans the workload's infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi, k8s manifests, or equivalent) for direct IAM grants on observability backend resources — AWS IAM policies or role attachments referencing `arn:aws:s3:::observability-*`, `arn:aws:logs:*:*:log-group:/aws/observability/*`, or equivalent; Datadog / Langsmith / Langfuse API tokens with read scope on observability orgs; cross-account observability role assumptions. Any grant present outside the central policy file fails the PR. The allow-list of central-managed observability resources lives at `observability/iam_managed_by_central.yaml`, maintained by the platform team and pinned with the central policy package version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central observability policy package at a version ≥ the floor declared in `observability/policy_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each access-policy or retention-schema cutover. Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated access-policy schemas from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO3B2-02 — Govern read access and retention on AI observability traces through a centrally-owned policy — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

All AI observability traces emitted via the central SDK (sibling principle GO3B2-01) are governed at read time by a centrally-owned access policy file keyed on the trace's compliance_tier (regulated / sensitive / standard), declared as code in the platform repository. A central retention policy file maps each compliance_tier to a maximum retention window and is enforced through backend lifecycle rules generated by platform-team-owned IaC. Project workloads declare their compliance_tier in their observability config but may not grant IAM read on observability backend resources directly — a pre-merge IaC lint blocks any project-local grant against the central allow-list. Every read of regulated- or sensitive-tier traces is captured in a read-side audit log fed into a central security queue. The platform/security team owns the policy files, the lifecycle IaC, the read-audit pipeline, and a backend-access review workflow with a one-week SLA.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is an audit-time-discovery failure that accumulates silently across team boundaries and surfaces at a discrete triggering moment with disproportionate retrofit cost. Multiple project teams have AI workloads emitting traces (the SDK from GO3B2-01 is in place — traces are correctly emitted, tagged with compliance_tier, and PII-pre-scrubbed). The fragmentation is on the read side. Each team configured its own IAM grants on its own backend at workload onboarding. Each team accepted the vendor default for retention (30 days on Datadog, 13 months on Langsmith, indefinite on CloudWatch if no log-group retention rule was set). Each team's backend defaulted read-access logging to off. Locally, each team's setup looked fine at onboarding: the people who needed access had it, retention was 'whatever the vendor gave us,' and nobody asked about read audit logs because there was no incident to investigate.

The drift accumulates invisibly over months or years. Contractors get added to incident-response roles on backend A but never to the corresponding role on backend B; when they offboard, backend A revokes them but backend B's role still resolves to the trace bucket. Engineers move teams and their old grants outlive their need-to-know. Backend retention quietly carries traces well past the compliance_tier window the workload's onboarding compliance review committed to. Nobody is doing anything wrong on any single PR — the system is drifting because nobody owns the read side centrally.

The failure surfaces at a discrete triggering moment: an external auditor asks 'list everyone who read regulated-tier traces last quarter' and the answer is 'we cannot reconstruct that'; a GDPR DSAR for a customer whose production data was deleted two years ago turns up four-year-old traces in a backend with no lifecycle rule; a cross-team incident requires a trace reconstruction the SRE on-call cannot perform because the IAM grant ticket takes three days and by the time it lands the live data has rolled past one team's retention window; a contractor offboarded six weeks ago is found in a SOC2 spot-check to have continued read access through a stale grant on a second backend. In every case the principle's value is forcing the read side to be governed centrally before the second backend or the second team is onboarded, so contractor offboarding, retention bounds, read-audit coverage, and cross-team incident-response access are emergent properties of one policy file rather than drift that surfaces in a SOC2 finding or a regulator's question.

APPLICABILITY

GO3B2-02 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads (wherever GO3B2-01 emits). It is nice-to-have for traditional ML workloads. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where rich trace data lands in observability backends, and the example below is illustrative of how the same audit-time-discovery failure would manifest inside an ML model-monitoring pipeline.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO3B2-02's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise that has multiple AI workloads in production, each emitting traces through the central SDK from GO3B2-01 (do not labour this — assume the reader knows the SDK is in place). Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three workloads owned by different teams, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the observability backends each team's traces land in (Datadog, Langsmith, Langfuse, CloudWatch, S3 archive — any plausible mix). Crucially: hint at the drift offhandedly — note that each team configured its own IAM grants at onboarding, that retention is 'whatever the vendor defaults to,' that read-access logging was never explicitly turned on, that contractors and team-movers have accumulated incidental access nobody has audited. The reader should picture a confident enterprise where emission is solved and read-side governance has never been a board-room conversation. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the discrete triggering moment when the accumulated drift surfaces. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the trigger (an external auditor asks 'who read regulated-tier traces last quarter'; a GDPR DSAR for a deleted customer turns up multi-year-old trace references in a backend with no retention rule; a cross-team incident reconstruction stalls on IAM tickets while live data rolls past retention; a SOC2 spot-check finds an offboarded contractor still holding read access on a second backend; a compliance officer discovers regulated traces are being retained 13 months when the SOC2 narrative committed to 30 days). Then describe what the enterprise tries to do and where it falls apart — pulling backend-by-backend access reports, reconciling IAM grants across vendors with different role models, reconstructing read history from logs that were never turned on. Name roles (the security engineer compiling the audit response; the privacy officer fielding the DSAR; the SRE on-call blocked from cross-team traces; the compliance officer realising the retention narrative does not match reality). Make the consequence concrete — an audit finding logged, a DSAR commitment that cannot be met, an incident reconstruction abandoned, a multi-quarter retrofit project to centralise access governance after the fact. The failure must demonstrate something a central access policy plus centrally-generated retention lifecycle rules plus read-side audit logging would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'we have too many IAM roles' problem."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The fraud-triage agent's traces in Langsmith' not 'the AI system's logs'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('about six teams', 'roughly four years of accumulated traces').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'an external auditor', 'a data-subject access request', 'a quarterly security audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

implementation_step.title: Select the most cost-efficient model based on the prioritized cost dimensions (like hosting paradigm, model size, or token cost)
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Select the most cost-efficient model based on the prioritized cost dimensions (like hosting paradigm, model size, or token cost).

references.note: AWS step 3 calls for the model selection to be made cost-aware against the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). AWS leaves unspecified: where the decision record lives, what shape it takes, what content elements it must capture, and how it is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI. This principle concretises all four. It also absorbs the substance of GENCOST01-BP01 steps 1 (Identify minimum performance requirements) and 2 (Determine models meeting the bar) as content elements WITHIN the decision record — those AWS steps are not_promoted as standalone principles in this catalogue (see lens_mapping.md GENCOST01-BP01 ledger).

statement.title: Maintain a versioned model-selection decision record in the workload repository declaring requirements, candidate models, chosen model, and cost-aware rationale
statement.description: Each foundation-model selection — initial choice or replacement — must be captured as a versioned decision record (ADR or equivalent) under the workload repository, naming the minimum performance requirements the model must meet, the candidate models evaluated against those requirements, the chosen model, and the cost-aware rationale tying the choice to the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost).

problem.description: Without a versioned model-selection decision record in the workload repository, the choice of foundation model lives in tribal knowledge — the senior engineer who set the workload up, a Slack thread, a notebook that was never committed. The failure surfaces not at selection time (the workload runs fine) but at the first exposure event that demands defence of the choice: finance flagging the bill, the original architect leaving, a cheaper model arriving, an audit asking for justification. At that point, the team cannot reconstruct what minimum bar was set, what alternatives were evaluated, or what cost-vs-quality trade-off was accepted — every comparison becomes a green-field decision instead of a revisitable one. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A team picks a frontier model for an insurance underwriting agent because the original architect prefers it; six months later finance flags the workload at roughly $80K/month and asks if a smaller model would suffice; nobody can produce the original minimum performance bar against which the choice was made, so the comparison is run from scratch.

A team uses a frontier model for a customer-service workflow that turns out to need only sentiment classification — a $0.30-per-call workload a 7B model could handle for fractions of a cent. The decision happened in a Slack thread three quarters ago and the cost ratio was never reconciled until volume scaled.

An external auditor asks the team to justify the model choice for a regulated decisioning workload; the original architect has moved teams; the new owner can produce no requirements spec, no evaluated alternatives, no decision rationale; the audit logs "model selection not documented" as a remediation item.

A new model class is released and the platform team needs to evaluate cost-vs-quality migration across ten workloads; for none of them can they find the original selection rationale, so every migration becomes a green-field decision rather than an update to a known prior choice.

A workload is on a self-hosted 70B model at ~$250/day uptime; a managed alternative would be ~$80/day at current token volume; the team cannot find who originally decided self-hosting was right or what assumptions (volume, latency, privacy) drove the call, so they cannot evaluate whether those assumptions still hold.

solution.approach: Maintain a versioned model-selection decision record at a known repository path (convention: `model-selection/decision.md` or equivalent ADR location under the workload repository). The record names: (1) the minimum performance requirements the model must meet (the criteria considered, absorbing the substance of AWS step 1); (2) the candidate models evaluated against those requirements (the shortlist, absorbing AWS step 2); (3) the chosen model; (4) the cost-aware rationale tying the choice to the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). Wire two pre-merge CI gates against the record: an existence + content-completeness gate, and a coupling gate that requires any PR adding or changing a model reference to update the decision record.
key_benefits: Foundational dependency for periodic re-evaluation (GC1B1-02) — the re-evaluation principle cannot fire usefully against a workload whose original selection rationale is missing; the decision record is the baseline that re-evaluation compares forward from.
Production knowledge survives team changes; the decision lives in the workload repository, not in any individual's head, Slack thread, or local notebook.

Cost-aware rationale tied to the three prioritized cost dimensions makes the choice defensible to finance and procurement at any review moment — the bill is no longer surprising because the cost basis is documented from day one.

The decision record absorbs the substance of AWS steps 1 (requirements) and 2 (candidates evaluated) as content elements within a single artefact, eliminating the proliferation of separate requirements specs and shortlist documents that would otherwise drift apart.

gates.check: On any PR, the workload repository must contain a versioned model-selection decision record (convention: `model-selection/decision.md` or equivalent), with all four required content elements present: minimum performance requirements considered, candidate models evaluated, chosen model, and cost-aware rationale referencing the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that adds, changes, or removes a foundation-model reference under `src/` (model identifier, vendor, hosting paradigm), the PR must also modify `model-selection/decision.md` OR include a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why no decision-record update is required. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC1B1-01 — Maintain a versioned model-selection decision record in the workload repository declaring requirements, candidate models, chosen model, and cost-aware rationale — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every foundation-model selection (initial choice or replacement) must be captured as a versioned decision record in the workload repository, naming the minimum performance requirements the model must meet, the candidate models evaluated against those requirements, the chosen model, and the cost-aware rationale tying the choice to the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). The record is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI: any PR that adds or changes a model reference must update it.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible at the first exposure event that demands defence of the model choice. The team has a workload in production using a foundation model the original architect picked. The choice was made informally — a Slack thread, a notebook, a hallway conversation — and never committed as a structured decision record. The workload runs; the choice is not actively wrong. The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: finance flags the workload's bill and asks if a smaller model would suffice; a cheaper model class is released and procurement wants a cost-vs-quality comparison; an auditor (internal compliance, external regulator, or platform-team review) asks the team to justify the choice; the original architect leaves and the new owner inherits a model whose selection rationale lives in nobody's head. At that moment the team cannot reconstruct what minimum bar was set, what alternatives were evaluated, or what trade-off was accepted. Every comparison becomes a green-field decision rather than a revisitable one. The consequence is a forced choice: stall the workload while the missing record is reconstructed from memory, defend the choice with rationale invented after the fact, or migrate without proper comparison and inherit the cost-of-being-wrong at production scale.

The principle's value is forcing the selection to be a first-class versioned artefact from day one — committed, with the four content elements, wired to CI so any model change touches it. Without it, the cost basis of every model decision lives in tribal knowledge and breaks the moment that knowledge moves on.

APPLICABILITY

GC1B1-01 is mandatory for LLM, RAG, and agentic workloads where the model is a foundation-model-as-a-service decision with real cost stakes. It is nice_to_have for classical ML workloads where model selection follows well-established statistical discipline. If the chosen Implementation type is not one of the mandatory three (or if it is ML), open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for foundation-model selection at production cost scale, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest in the chosen pattern.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC1B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload that is in production with a chosen foundation model. Be specific about the industry, the implementation type, the team, the workload's role, and the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Name the model that was chosen (use a plausible model class — frontier vs mid-tier vs small-tier — without fabricating specific commercial model names where avoidable). Crucially: name how the choice was made — the informal mechanism (a Slack thread, a notebook the original architect ran on their laptop, a hallway agreement at workload kick-off). The reader should picture a confident team unaware that the selection record does not survive contact with change. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the missing selection record becomes a problem. Walk through chronologically: name the triggering event (finance escalation on the bill; the original architect leaving; a cheaper model class released; a procurement review; an internal compliance audit; a quarterly model-risk review). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot produce the original requirements bar, cannot list the alternatives that were considered, cannot defend the cost-vs-quality trade-off that justified the chosen paradigm (managed vs self-hosted; larger vs smaller model). Make the consequence concrete: a forced green-field comparison that stalls a release; a migration that ships without proper cost comparison; a compliance finding that opens a remediation backlog; finance pulling budget while the selection rationale is reconstructed. The failure must demonstrate something a committed, four-element decision record with CI coupling would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'the model performed badly' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage workload' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.

references.note: AWS step 4 calls for the hosting infrastructure to be right-sized to the workload's demand profile with the most cost-optimized configuration that meets performance requirements. AWS leaves unspecified: where the right-sizing baseline lives, where utilization alarms are declared, how the declared baseline is enforced against actual deployed infrastructure (the most common bypass), and how reserved-capacity / scheduling / quantization decisions are made auditable. This principle concretises all four — `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` as the baseline artefact, `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` as the alarms artefact, two pre-merge CI gates (existence + content; code-vs-record consistency lint comparing IaC against the YAML and verifying deployed alarms), and runtime alarm enforcement post-deploy. The principle absorbs the substance of GENCOST02-BP02 steps 1 (demand identification — content WITHIN the YAML's utilization_targets), 2 (deploy on acceptable infrastructure — pre-condition), and 3 (establish demand profile — observation work product absorbed by the right-sizing config's baseline). AWS's implementation guidance is heavily vendor-menu (SageMaker AI HyperPod, EKS, Slurm, EC2 Reserved Instances, Inference Recommender, quantization techniques) but the step wording itself is a generic mandate; vendor specifics belong here in framework_mappings notes rather than in the principle's solution.approach.

statement.title: Right-size hosting infrastructure to the workload's demand profile and commit utilization alarms as code
statement.description: Each workload that self-hosts a foundation model for inference (or runs on committed managed capacity such as Bedrock Provisioned Throughput) must declare a versioned right-sizing baseline and a versioned set of utilization alarms in the workload repository, both as code. The baseline names the target utilization band per resource (GPU / CPU / memory), the instance ceiling, the scheduling and idle policy, the reserved-capacity coverage target as a function of workload age, and the model identifier and quantization precision. The alarms fire when production utilization, scheduling discipline, reserved-capacity coverage, or quantization-review cadence drift from the declared baseline. The actual deployed infrastructure must match the declared baseline — divergence requires an explicit ADR, not silent drift.

problem.description: Without utilization alarms committed as code and a right-sizing baseline tied to the workload's observed demand profile, oversized hosting infrastructure runs invisibly — the workload performs correctly, just expensively. No per-PR CI gate catches this because nothing in the PR is wrong; the wrong choice was made at first deploy or has drifted since. The only signal is the monthly bill, which arrives 30 days late, lands with finance not engineering, and triggers an audit that takes weeks to land an optimization PR. Meanwhile the workload continues paying the premium. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: Self-hosted Llama runs on `ml.g5.12xlarge` sized for projected peak; actual production peaks at 18% GPU utilization; no alarm declared in `infra/monitoring/`; engineering only notices when finance flags the workload during quarterly cost review six months in.

Inference endpoint runs 24/7 on a workload whose traffic is bounded to UK business hours; no scheduled-scaling or shutdown policy committed; the workload spends 70% of compute time idle, with no signal raised because the workload itself is healthy.
Workload runs 18 months past first deploy with no Reserved Instance or Savings Plan coverage; on-demand premium accumulates; no alarm fires against `RI coverage < workload-age threshold`.
Self-hosted model deployed at FP16 on a model class that supports INT8 quantization with negligible quality impact; quantization is never applied because no alarm declares the eligibility check.

Reserved-capacity purchase sized to projected demand 18 months ago; actual usage has stayed at 40% of reserved; no alarm declares a coverage-vs-usage check; the reserved overspend is invisible because RIs look like a cost optimization on the surface.

solution.approach: Commit a hosting-infrastructure right-sizing config at a known path in the workload repository (convention: `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml`), declaring: (1) the right-sizing baseline — target utilization band per resource (GPU / CPU / memory), instance ceiling, scheduling windows where the workload may shut down, and reserved-capacity coverage target as a function of workload age; (2) utilization alarms as code (convention: `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml`), each alarm naming the resource, the metric, the threshold, the evaluation window, and the alert routing. Wire two pre-merge CI gates: one enforces existence + content-completeness on both files; the other is a code-vs-record consistency lint that parses the workload's IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation, k8s manifests, docker-compose — whichever the workload uses) and compares declared GPU instance count, GPU type, model identifier, and quantization precision against the YAML's corresponding fields, AND verifies that every alarm declared in the YAML is actually deployed via the same IaC as an active monitoring resource. The PR fails if any mismatch is present without an accompanying ADR. The alarms themselves do the runtime enforcement once deployed — they fire when actual production utilization drifts outside the declared band, when reserved-capacity coverage expires, or when scheduled shutdown windows are violated.
key_benefits: Catches the silent-waste failure mode that monthly bills surface 30 days late and aggregate cost reports cannot attribute — alarms fire at the workload level the moment utilization drifts outside the declared band, not at billing close.

Builds on GC1B1-01 without overlap: GC1B1-01 records the paradigm/model choice gated on `src/` changes; GC2B2-01 enforces within-paradigm right-sizing gated on `infra/` changes. Different file scope, different lifecycle moment, complementary coverage.

Code-vs-record consistency lint (Gate 2) closes the bypass loophole that pure documentation-discipline principles cannot — declared baseline is binding against deployed infrastructure, not honour-system, so the YAML cannot quietly drift from reality.

Workload-age-anchored reserved-capacity coverage check forces the RI / Savings Plan decision rather than leaving it as an indefinite open item; long-running workloads with no commitment surface as alarm-eligible.

Workload-agnostic across in-scope serving paradigms — declare baseline, declare alarms, gate on infra-config drift — applies whether self-hosted on managed inference endpoints, self-hosted on unmanaged clusters, or on committed-capacity managed APIs (where the commitment IS a right-sizing decision); only the specific resources and thresholds differ.

gates.check: On any PR, `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` and `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` must both exist and be content-complete. The right-sizing config must declare: `resources.gpu` (type, count, ceiling), `utilization_targets` (compute and memory bands per session state), `scheduling` (active_windows, idle_policy with auto_suspend_after_idle and full_shutdown_after_idle, weekly_max_active_hours), and `model` (identifier, precision, precision_review_due). The alarms config must declare at minimum a gpu_compute_oversized alarm, an idle_shutdown_violated alarm, and a quantization_review_overdue alarm. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, a lint must parse the workload's infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, CloudFormation, k8s manifests, docker-compose, or equivalent) and verify two things: (a) the declared hosting infrastructure matches `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` — actual GPU instance count must be ≤ `resources.gpu.ceiling`; actual GPU type must equal `resources.gpu.type`; actual served model identifier must equal `model.identifier`; actual quantization precision must equal `model.precision`; (b) every alarm declared in `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` must be deployed via the same IaC as an active monitoring resource (CloudWatch alarm, Prometheus rule, or equivalent depending on the workload's monitoring stack). The lint fails the PR if any mismatch is present without an accompanying ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying the temporary divergence. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Decomposition: GENCOST02-BP01 closed 0 promoted (all four steps absorbed by GC1B1-01 which already covers hosting-paradigm choice in its model-selection ADR); GENCOST02-BP02 decomposition under the step_promotion rubric: steps 1/2/3 not_promoted (decision-process advice / pre-condition / observation work products); step 4 promoted as this principle. Statement: 'Right-size hosting infrastructure to the workload's demand profile and commit utilization alarms as code' — derives from AWS step 4's operative verbs (Optimize, select) rather than the documentation-shaped 'maintain a decision record' interpretation initially considered. Two pre_merge CI gates, both required status checks on the integration branch: (1) existence + content-completeness on right-sizing config + utilization alarms YAMLs; (2) code-vs-record consistency lint comparing IaC against the YAML baselines AND verifying deployed alarm presence — closes the documentation-discipline bypass that GC1B1-01 acknowledged but deliberately left open. impact_level Medium (matches AWS BP rating; cost failures recoverable via re-optimization, parallel with GC1B1-01's principled outlier from the GO1B1 family's High). applicability { llm/rag/agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have } matching GC1B1-01's broad shape. serving_paradigm { self_hosted_managed: mandatory, self_hosted_unmanaged: mandatory, api_provisioned: nice_to_have } — api_on_demand omitted (no infrastructure to right-size); api_provisioned nice_to_have because commitment sizing IS a right-sizing decision but provider handles underlying instance optimization. maturity_level scaling — three reasons: (1) Gate 2's code-vs-record consistency lint is real platform-team engineering (IaC parser, cross-reference logic, cloud-API verification of deployed alarms), not first-project capability; (2) the principle only applies to self-hosted or committed-managed workloads per serving_paradigm, and self-hosting is rarely a first AI project choice — teams typically start on API on-demand and migrate to self-hosted only at scaling tier when cost or data-residency pressures arrive; (3) runtime alarm enforcement needs a deployed instance to fire against, same logic that put GO1B1-04 (drift monitor) at scaling.
