explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC2B2-01 — Right-size hosting infrastructure to the workload's demand profile and commit utilization alarms as code — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every workload that self-hosts a foundation model for inference (or runs on committed managed capacity such as Bedrock Provisioned Throughput) must declare a versioned right-sizing baseline and a versioned set of utilization alarms in the workload repository, both as code. The baseline names the target utilization band per resource (GPU / CPU / memory), instance ceiling, scheduling and idle policy, reserved-capacity coverage target as a function of workload age, and the model identifier and quantization precision. The alarms fire when production utilization, scheduling discipline, reserved-capacity coverage, or quantization-review cadence drift from the declared baseline. CI gates enforce: (a) both YAMLs exist and are content-complete; (b) the actual deployed infrastructure-as-code matches the YAML — declared GPU count / type / model / precision must equal what IaC deploys, and every declared alarm must be deployed as an active monitoring resource.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible at the first exposure event that demands defence of the hosting cost. The team has a workload running in production on self-hosted infrastructure. The original architect picked an instance size when first deploying — informed by projected peak load, rough back-of-envelope estimates, or simply 'this is what the spike chart said we'd need.' The workload runs fine. The instance is oversized for the actual production load; the workload runs 24/7 even though traffic only hits business hours; no Reserved Instance was ever purchased because the team meant to evaluate it later; the model was deployed at FP16 even though the model class supports INT8 with negligible quality loss. None of these are visible from inside the workload — the workload is healthy. The bill is the only signal, and the bill is 30 days late, lands with finance not engineering, and gets absorbed as 'AI infrastructure cost.'

The absence becomes visible at a triggering event: a quarterly cost review surfaces the workload as a top-N spend item; an internal audit asks why the workload runs on the instance class it does; the original architect leaves and the new owner inherits an instance whose sizing rationale lives in nobody's head; a procurement review asks if Reserved Instances would help and nobody can produce the utilization data to make the call; a release notes scan flags that INT8 quantization is widely supported now and the team has not evaluated it. At that moment the team cannot reconstruct what utilization band was assumed, what scheduling policy was supposed to be in place, what reserved-capacity coverage was intended. Every optimization becomes a green-field investigation rather than an alarm-driven micro-decision. The consequence is months of accumulated overspend that compounds with each quarter the discipline does not exist.

The principle's value is forcing the right-sizing baseline AND its enforcement to be first-class versioned artefacts from day one — committed, with explicit utilization bands and scheduling policies and reserved-capacity targets, AND with a CI gate that verifies actual deployed IaC matches the declaration. Without both, the discipline is honour-system: the YAML can exist while the deployed infrastructure quietly drifts past it.

APPLICABILITY

GC2B2-01 is mandatory for workloads self-hosted on managed infrastructure (SageMaker AI endpoints, Vertex AI endpoints, Azure ML managed endpoints) or on unmanaged infrastructure (EC2 + vLLM, EKS / Slurm / HyperPod clusters, on-prem GPUs), and nice_to_have for workloads on committed managed-API capacity (Bedrock Provisioned Throughput, Azure OpenAI PTUs) where the commitment-sizing decision IS a right-sizing decision but the provider handles the underlying instance optimization. The principle does not apply at all to pure-API on-demand workloads (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock on-demand) — there is no hosting infrastructure to right-size; cost optimisation in that paradigm is at the prompt layer (GENCOST03). If the chosen Implementation type's typical serving paradigm is api_on_demand, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting GC2B2-01 does not apply to pure-API workloads and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest if the team self-hosted instead.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC2B2-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload self-hosted (or on committed managed capacity) in production. Be specific about the industry, the implementation type, the team, the workload's role, and the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Name the foundation model in use (use a plausible open-weight model class — Llama / Mistral / Qwen — or a committed-managed equivalent) and the hosting choice (an instance size for self-hosted, a committed-throughput unit for managed). Crucially: name how the instance/commitment was sized — the informal mechanism (the original architect's back-of-envelope, the projected peak load from launch planning, a copy of another workload's sizing). The reader should picture a confident team unaware that the sizing rationale and the enforcement around it do not survive contact with production reality. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the missing right-sizing baseline and alarms become a problem. Walk through chronologically: name the triggering event (quarterly cost review surfacing the workload as a top-N spend; internal audit asking why the chosen instance class; the original architect leaving; procurement review asking about Reserved Instances; release notes flagging new quantization variants). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot produce the utilization band that was assumed, cannot show what scheduling policy was supposed to be in place, cannot defend the reserved-capacity coverage decision, cannot tell whether the workload is over-sized or under-utilised because no alarm tells them. Make the consequence concrete: months of accumulated overspend; finance pulling budget; a green-field re-investigation that stalls a release; a Reserved Instance purchase made on guesswork rather than data. The failure must demonstrate something a committed right-sizing baseline + utilization alarms + IaC-consistency lint would have caught at the moment of drift — not a generic 'the bill was too high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage workload' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly $25K/month overspend', 'about a quarter before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's infrastructure audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

implementation_step.title: Engineer the prompt to reduce the token count
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Engineer the prompt to reduce the token count, trimming as many unnecessary words as possible.

references.note: AWS GENCOST03-BP01 'Optimize prompt token length' tells teams to reduce prompt token count (step 2: 'trimming as many unnecessary words as possible') but leaves the discipline as coaching — it specifies no artefact, no ceiling, and no gate. This principle concretises step 2 into an enforceable contract: a declared per-template input-token ceiling (reusing GO3B1-01's runtime_token_budget.input field) plus a pre-merge gate that computes the template's declared-worst-case footprint and fails the build when it exceeds the ceiling, plus a budget-inflation governance gate. It absorbs step 1 ('Identify a verbose prompt which could be optimized') — a template that breaches its ceiling IS the identified verbose prompt, surfaced mechanically rather than by manual hunting. Steps 3 (use a separate LLM / Amazon Bedrock Prompt Optimization to shorten) and 4 (zero-shot / chain-of-thought / tree-of-thought / least-to-most technique menu plus 'continue testing') are not_promoted: step 3 is a vendor/technique suggestion with no architectural mandate surviving extraction, step 4 is a prompt-engineering technique menu plus process advice. The exact GENCOST03 question-stem wording is approximated from the verified focus-area title 'Cost-aware prompting' pending a question-page verification pass; the BP title and step-2 verbatim_text are verified against the live AWS Lens page (fetched 2026-06-04). Vendor specifics (Amazon Bedrock Prompt Optimization) are kept here rather than in solution.approach.

references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — a declared, enforced prompt-token budget is a design-and-build-time governance control over the model-call cost surface, aligning with III.B's lifecycle scope. Same anchor as cost siblings GC1B1-01 and GC2B2-01. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Cap every prompt template at a declared token budget and fail builds whose token footprint exceeds it
statement.description: Every registered prompt template must declare an input-token ceiling (`runtime_token_budget.input` in the GO3B1-01 manifest) that reflects a real cap, not a placeholder. A pre-merge gate computes each template's declared-worst-case input footprint — the static scaffolding tokenised with the model's own tokenizer plus the sum of its declared per-variable ceilings (`typical_max`) — and fails the build when that footprint exceeds the declared budget, or when the budget is raised without a recorded cost rationale. This converts AWS's aspirational 'reduce the token count' into an auditable ceiling keyed on `template_id`, so prompt-length cost growth is caught at review time instead of surfacing on the inference bill.

problem.description: Without an enforced input-token ceiling per prompt template, prompt scaffolding grows unreviewed — an extra few-shot exemplar here, an expanded system instruction there, a widened retrieval-context ceiling — and the cost lands only on the inference bill: thirty days late, attributed to finance rather than engineering, with no review moment that flags 'this template just got 40% more expensive per call.' GO3B1-01 already requires every template to declare a `runtime_token_budget.input`, but nothing checks that number against the template's actual footprint — it is set once at registration and never enforced, so it drifts into fiction while the prompt underneath it bloats. The failure is cumulative: no single PR is wrong, each token addition is locally reasonable, and the aggregate cost regression is invisible to per-PR review and to every other cost gate, because model-selection and hosting right-sizing do not look at prompt length. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A triage classifier template's system prompt grows from roughly 200 to 900 tokens across three separate PRs as engineers append edge-case instructions; each PR reads as a reasonable fix in isolation; the manifest's declared `runtime_token_budget.input: 400` is never compared against the body, so the ceiling is fiction and per-call input cost more than doubles before anyone notices at the quarterly cost review.

A RAG summarisation template's `context` variable has its `typical_max` bumped from 2,000 to 8,000 tokens to fix a truncation bug; the one-line manifest edit ships uncontested; nobody computes that the template's declared-worst-case input now exceeds its `runtime_token_budget.input` threefold, and the cost only surfaces a quarter later when retrieval volume scales.

A few-shot extraction template carries twelve exemplars where three would meet the quality bar; the nine redundant exemplars add several thousand input tokens to every call; no gate compares the assembled scaffolding against the declared ceiling, so the waste is invisible to CI and shows up only as an unexplained line on the inference bill.

A team raises a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` from 1,000 to 6,000 in the very same PR that introduces a verbose new prompt body, because nothing requires a recorded rationale for inflating a budget; the ceiling silently tracks the bloat instead of constraining it, and the change passes review as a routine manifest edit.

Finance attributes a 30% inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth'; the real driver is prompt-scaffolding creep across six templates; the workload's GC1B1-01 model-selection ADR and GC2B2-01 hosting right-sizing both pass cleanly — neither looks at prompt length — so the actual cost driver is invisible to every existing cost gate and to GO3B1-01's existence-only budget check.

solution.approach: The baseline mandate (Option A — declared-worst-case, manifest-derivable) reuses the prompt registry GO3B1-01 already requires: every template's input ceiling lives in the existing `runtime_token_budget.input` field of `prompts/manifest.yaml` (Mode A) or the TMS metadata (Mode B), and it must hold a real cap rather than a placeholder. A central lint (shipped as part of the GO3B1-01 llm-sdk lint suite; convention `prompts/budget_check`) computes each template's declared-worst-case input footprint = static scaffolding tokens (the body at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, `{{variable}}` placeholders excluded) + the sum of each declared variable's `typical_max`, and fails the PR when that footprint exceeds the declared `runtime_token_budget.input` or when the budget is absent or zero. A second lint governs budget inflation: any PR raising a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` or a variable's `typical_max` above its previous committed value must carry a recorded cost rationale (a manifest `change_history` entry or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/`), so a ceiling cannot be silently widened to absorb bloat. Both gates are manifest-derivable — no fixtures, no deployed baseline, no runtime — so they fire at review time. Two alternative reference implementations are documented for workloads that want tighter coupling to real cost and MAY be adopted in addition to (not instead of) the Option A baseline: **Option B (measured-from-fixtures)** runs each template through the workload's GO1B1 eval-harness fixtures, measures the assembled input tokens with the model tokenizer, and gates the measured typical footprint against the declared budget — closer to real cost, at the price of coupling to fixture representativeness; **Option C (runtime alarm)** wires GO3B1-01's existing per-call actual-vs-declared token signal into an alarm that pages or dashboard-flags when production input tokens exceed the declared budget over a window — catches pathological runtime fills Options A and B cannot see, but is post-deploy observability rather than a pre-merge gate, so it complements rather than replaces the CI gate. The reference implementation at `data/ri/GC3B1-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Turns GO3B1-01's declared-but-toothless `runtime_token_budget.input` into a binding ceiling — the silent scaffolding creep that today surfaces only on the inference bill thirty days late now fails the PR that introduces it.

Manifest-derivable and deterministic — the gate reuses GO3B1-01's tokenizer integration and the per-variable `typical_max` ceilings already in the manifest, so it bites at review time with no fixtures, no deployed baseline, and no runtime dependency.
The budget-inflation governance gate closes the dodge loophole: a ceiling cannot be quietly widened to track bloat, because every upward change leaves an auditable cost-rationale trail.

Builds on GO3B1-01 without overlap and covers the cost gates' blind spot — GO3B1-01 mandates the budget field exists, GC1B1-01 records the model choice, GC2B2-01 right-sizes hosting; none of them constrain prompt length, which is the cost driver this principle catches (different failure mode, different focus area).

Paradigm-neutral by construction — because prompt length drives cost whether the model is billed per token or via self-hosted compute time (AWS GENCOST03-BP01 verbatim), the same declared-ceiling gate applies across all four serving paradigms, and parameterises cleanly over both GO3B1-01 storage modes (manifest sum for Mode A, TMS metadata for Mode B).

gates.check: On any PR, a lint computes each registered prompt template's declared-worst-case input footprint and fails the build when it exceeds the template's declared `runtime_token_budget.input`. The footprint is computed as the template body's static scaffolding token count — the body at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` (Mode A) or the TMS-resolved body (Mode B), tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, with `{{variable}}` placeholders excluded — PLUS the sum of each declared variable's `typical_max` from the manifest. The lint also fails the PR for any template whose `runtime_token_budget.input` is absent or zero, since a real ceiling rather than a placeholder is required. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that raises a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` or any variable's `typical_max` above its previous committed value (diffed against the merge base), the PR must also add a budget-change rationale — either an entry in the template's `change_history` within the manifest row, or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` — recording why the ceiling was raised and the per-call input-token cost delta. A lint detects any upward budget or `typical_max` change without an accompanying rationale entry and fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC3B1-01 — Cap every prompt template at a declared token budget and fail builds whose token footprint exceeds it — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every registered prompt template declares an input-token ceiling (the runtime_token_budget.input field GO3B1-01 already requires in the prompt manifest) that must hold a real cap, not a placeholder. A pre-merge gate computes each template's declared-worst-case input footprint — the static scaffolding tokenised with the model's own tokenizer plus the sum of its declared per-variable typical_max ceilings — and fails the build when that footprint exceeds the declared budget. A second gate requires a recorded cost rationale whenever a budget or a variable ceiling is raised, so the ceiling cannot be silently widened to absorb bloat. The mandate is manifest-derivable (no fixtures, no deployed baseline) and keyed on template_id.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a cumulative-drift failure: many individually-reasonable token additions compound, across PRs and across templates, into a portfolio-level cost regression that no single review catches. A team has prompt templates registered (the GO3B1-01 registry is in place — templates are addressable, each carries a declared runtime_token_budget.input). The budgets were set once at registration and never enforced. Over the following months, engineers do locally-rational things: append an edge-case instruction to a system prompt, add two more few-shot exemplars to lift a quality metric, widen a retrieval-context variable ceiling to fix a truncation bug, paste in a longer policy preamble. Each change ships in a PR that reads as a reasonable fix; each adds a few hundred or a few thousand input tokens; none is individually wrong. The declared budget drifts into fiction while the prompt underneath it bloats, and because model-selection and hosting-cost gates never look at prompt length, every other cost control passes clean.

The drift is invisible until a triggering moment forces the aggregate into view: a quarterly cost review flags the workload as a top-N spend item; finance attributes a double-digit inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth' and asks engineering to explain it; a migration to a model with a smaller context window forces a prompt audit that discovers templates running several times their declared ceiling; a per-template token-cost dashboard finally attributes spend and the scaffolding creep becomes legible. At that moment the team cannot point to the PR that 'caused' the regression because no single PR did — the cost is the sum of dozens of reasonable edits across several templates, and there was never a review gate that compared a template's footprint against the ceiling it was supposed to respect.

The principle's value is forcing the ceiling to bind at review time, so prompt-length cost growth fails the PR that introduces it rather than accumulating silently onto the inference bill.

APPLICABILITY

GC3B1-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads — anywhere prompts exist as registered templates with a token footprint. It is nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads, which have no prompt-shaped artefact. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where prompt length is a direct cost driver, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same cumulative-drift cost failure would manifest in the closest analogue (for example unbounded feature-text or context assembly feeding an inference call).

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC3B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with one or more AI workloads in production whose prompts are registered as versioned templates (assume the GO3B1-01 registry is in place; do not labour it). Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three templates owned by a team, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the kind of content each template carries (a system instruction block, few-shot exemplars, a retrieval-context variable). Crucially: hint at the drift offhandedly — note that the declared token budgets were set at registration and never revisited, that engineers routinely tweak prompt bodies to fix quality issues, that nobody watches per-template token footprint. The reader should picture a confident team where the registry exists and prompt cost has never been a review-time concern. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the accumulated prompt-length cost drift surfaces. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (a quarterly cost review flags the workload; finance attributes an inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth'; a model migration to a smaller context window forces a prompt audit; a per-template token-cost dashboard finally attributes spend). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot point to a single causing PR because the regression is the sum of dozens of reasonable edits across multiple templates; they discover declared budgets that bear no relation to actual footprint; they find a variable ceiling that was widened a quarter ago and never costed. Name roles (the platform engineer building the cost dashboard; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who widened the context ceiling). Make the consequence concrete — months of accumulated per-call overspend across the portfolio, a scramble to retroactively trim prompts, a budget that turns out to be fiction. The failure must demonstrate something a declared-ceiling gate that computes footprint at review time would have caught at the PR that introduced each increment — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, templates. 'The claims-triage classifier template' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 4,000 extra tokens per call', 'about a quarter before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's cost audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

references.note: AWS BP03 verbatim and step list fetched from the live AWS Lens page 2026-06-05. Anchors step 2 (enable caching + configure cache checkpoints) and absorbs step 1 (Identify opportunities for caching — review for repeated prompt components, verify minimum-token thresholds) as the eligibility computation WITHIN the gate: a template whose computed static prefix clears the model's minimum cache size IS the identified caching opportunity, surfaced mechanically rather than by manual review. AWS leaves unspecified: where the cacheable-prefix size is declared, how eligibility is decided against the model's minimum-token threshold, how the declaration is kept honest against the actual prompt body, and how this is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI. This principle concretises all four. AWS's implementation guidance is Bedrock-specific (console toggle, Bedrock cache checkpoints) but the underlying mandate — mark the stable leading prefix of a reused prompt as a cache checkpoint so it bills at the provider's reduced cached-token rate — is provider-neutral (Anthropic `cache_control`, OpenAI automatic prefix caching, Bedrock cache checkpoints all implement the same prefix-reuse contract); vendor specifics are deferred to this note. Steps 3 (Monitor caching metrics — hit/miss rates, cached-vs-uncached cost) and 4 (Optimize cache usage — tune checkpoint placement, balance write vs read cost) are not_promoted as standalone principles: step 3's runtime monitoring is captured as the optional Option C (telemetry alarm on cache hit-rate) documented in solution.approach and properly belongs to the GO3B2 observability family; step 4 is tuning/process advice with no commitable pre-merge artefact. statement.title: Mark the cacheable static prefix of every reused prompt template and fail builds that leave a cache-eligible template uncached or mis-declare its prefix size
statement.description: Every registered prompt template must declare, in the GO3B1-01 manifest, the token count of its static prefix — the contiguous fixed leading span of the prompt before the first runtime variable — together with a cache decision. Any template whose declared static-prefix token count clears its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size must mark a cache checkpoint, so the reused prefix bills at the provider's reduced cached-token rate, or carry a recorded opt-out. A pre-merge gate fails the build when the static-prefix count is undeclared, when a cache-eligible template is neither cached nor opted out, or when the declared prefix count diverges from the value CI recomputes with the model's own tokenizer. This converts AWS's 'implement prompt caching' from an optional console toggle into an enforced, auditable property of every template, so the cheap-by-default savings on large reused prefixes are taken by construction rather than left on the inference bill.

problem.description: Prompt caching bills the stable, reused leading portion of a prompt at a steeply reduced rate (often a fraction of the standard input-token price) once it is marked as a cache checkpoint. Without a discipline that forces eligible templates to be marked, caching is simply never turned on: the workload runs fine, the output is correct, and the only signal that a large fixed prefix is being re-processed and billed at full rate on every call is the inference bill — thirty days late, attributed to finance, absorbed as 'model usage.' The discipline is also fragile in two further ways: a cacheable prefix silently breaks when a variable is inserted ahead of it, and a declared prefix size drifts into fiction when the body changes underneath it or the number is hand-written to pass a review. None of these are visible to per-PR review, and no other cost gate looks at prompt-prefix structure — model-selection and hosting right-sizing are blind to it. Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A RAG support assistant prepends a roughly 6,000-token product manual to every query as a stable system block; caching is never enabled, so the manual is re-processed and billed at full input rate across all ~50K daily calls; the waste surfaces only at the quarterly cost review, when someone realises the unchanging manual could have billed at the reduced cached-token rate from day one.

A claims-triage agent carries a roughly 3,000-token policy preamble that is byte-identical on every call; no cache checkpoint is declared; the team pays full input price for that preamble on every invocation for two quarters until a cost audit asks why a static preamble is billed fresh each time.

A template that was cacheable gets a `{{customer_name}}` personalisation variable inserted near the top during a feature PR; everything after it is now uncacheable; the cache hit rate silently collapses and per-call cost climbs, with no review flag because nothing checks that the stable prefix is still contiguous and ahead of the first variable.

A team declares a cache checkpoint but the static prefix is only about 600 tokens — below the model's ~1,024-token minimum — so caching never actually engages; nobody computed the prefix size, so the team believes caching is saving money while every call bills at full rate.

A developer hand-writes `static_prefix_tokens: 4000` in the manifest to clear a cost review, but the real fixed prefix is about 900 tokens because most of the 'fixed' text is actually filled per call; the declared number is fiction, the expected savings never materialise, and no gate recomputes the prefix against the tokenised body to catch the discrepancy.

solution.approach: The baseline mandate (Option A — declared-and-recomputed, manifest-derivable) reuses the prompt registry GO3B1-01 already requires. Each template's manifest row (Mode A: `prompts/manifest.yaml`; Mode B: TMS metadata) carries a `cache` block: `static_prefix_tokens` (the declared token count of the contiguous fixed leading span before the first `{{variable}}`) and a cache decision — either `checkpoint: true` (a cache checkpoint is marked at the end of the static prefix via the central SDK's provider-neutral caching contract) or `opt_out` with a recorded reason. A central lint (shipped in the GO3B1-01 llm-sdk lint suite; convention `prompts/cache_check`) enforces three things at pre-merge: (1) `cache.static_prefix_tokens` is declared for every template and fails when absent; (2) for any template whose declared `static_prefix_tokens` is greater than or equal to its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size — read from a central, platform-owned `models/cache_minimums.yaml` keyed by model identifier — the row must set `checkpoint: true` or carry a non-empty `opt_out` reason, and the build fails otherwise; (3) the lint recomputes the static prefix itself (the contiguous body span at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` up to the first `{{variable}}`, tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, reusing GO3B1-01's per-provider tokenizer adapters) and fails when the declared `static_prefix_tokens` diverges from the recomputed value beyond a declared tolerance (default 10%), which closes both the silent-break case (a variable inserted ahead of the prefix shrinks the computed prefix) and the hand-written-fiction case. All three checks are manifest-derivable — no fixtures, no deployed baseline, no runtime — so they fire at review time. One alternative reference implementation is documented for workloads wanting runtime assurance and MAY be adopted in addition to (not instead of) the Option A baseline: **Option C (runtime telemetry alarm)** wires the cache hit-rate and the actual production prefix size — already observable through GO3B2-01's central observability stream — into an alarm that dashboard-flags or pages when a cache-eligible template's production hit rate falls below a threshold or its actual prefix diverges from the declared value. Option C is post-deploy observability, not a pre-merge gate; it catches runtime cache misses (TTL expiry under low traffic, non-contiguous fills) that Option A cannot see, and properly belongs to the GO3B2 observability family, so it complements rather than replaces the CI gate. The reference implementation at `data/ri/GC3B3-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Takes the cheap-by-default savings by construction — a large reused prefix that today bills at full input rate on every call cannot merge until it either carries a cache checkpoint or a recorded opt-out, so the saving is the default rather than something a team has to remember to turn on.

The recompute gate stops the declared prefix count from drifting into fiction — a hand-written number that does not match the tokenised body fails the build, closing the gaming loophole the same way GC3B1-01's footprint gate closes the budget-as-placeholder loophole.

Catches the silent cache-break: inserting a variable ahead of the stable prefix shrinks the recomputed prefix, which the gate surfaces at the PR that introduces it instead of as an unexplained per-call cost rise a quarter later.

Catches false eligibility — a declared checkpoint on a sub-minimum prefix (where caching never actually engages) is flagged because the gate compares the recomputed prefix against the model's minimum cache size in `models/cache_minimums.yaml`.

Distinct from GC3B1-01 without overlap and reuses its machinery — GC3B1-01 fails prompts whose footprint is too BIG (creep past a ceiling); GC3B3-01 acts when a big prefix is STABLE and reused (mark it cacheable). Opposite lever on the same token cost, different failure mode (waste-by-not-caching vs creep-past-ceiling), both manifest-derivable on GO3B1-01's registry and tokenizer.

gates.check: On any PR, a lint verifies that every registered prompt template declares `cache.static_prefix_tokens` in its manifest row (Mode A `prompts/manifest.yaml`; Mode B TMS metadata) and fails the build when the field is absent. For any template whose declared `cache.static_prefix_tokens` is greater than or equal to its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size (read from the central, platform-owned `models/cache_minimums.yaml` keyed by the manifest `model` field), the row must set `cache.checkpoint: true` or carry a non-empty `cache.opt_out` reason; the build fails for a cache-eligible template that is neither cached nor opted out. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, a lint recomputes each template's static prefix — the contiguous body span at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` (Mode A) or the TMS-resolved body (Mode B) up to the first `{{variable}}`, tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field — and fails the build when the declared `cache.static_prefix_tokens` diverges from the recomputed value beyond the declared tolerance (default 10%). This catches both a prefix silently broken by a variable inserted ahead of it (recomputed prefix shrinks) and a hand-declared count that does not match the body. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC3B3-01 — Mark the cacheable static prefix of every reused prompt template and fail builds that leave a cache-eligible template uncached or mis-declare its prefix size — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every registered prompt template declares, in the GO3B1-01 manifest, the token count of its static prefix (the contiguous fixed leading span before the first runtime variable) and a cache decision. Any template whose static prefix clears its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size must mark a cache checkpoint — so the reused prefix bills at the provider's reduced cached-token rate — or carry a recorded opt-out. A pre-merge gate fails the build when the prefix count is undeclared, when a cache-eligible template is neither cached nor opted out, or when the declared prefix count diverges from the value CI recomputes with the model's own tokenizer.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure: prompt caching is simply never turned on for prompts that are eligible for it, and the absence is invisible until the bill is examined. The team has prompts registered (the GO3B1-01 registry is in place — templates are addressable, bodies are versioned). Many templates carry a large, byte-identical leading block reused on every call: a product manual prepended to every RAG query, a long policy preamble in an agent's system prompt, a fixed instruction-and-schema header. Caching that block would bill it at a fraction of the standard input rate. But nobody marked a cache checkpoint, because nothing required it; the prompt works, the output is correct, and the full-rate reprocessing of the stable prefix on every call shows up only as an undifferentiated line on the inference bill — thirty days late, owned by finance, absorbed as 'model usage growth.'

The absence is forced into view by a triggering event: a quarterly cost review flags the workload as a top-N spend item; a finance partner asks why a static preamble is billed fresh on every call; a migration audit examining prompt structure finds large reused prefixes that were never cached; a per-template cost dashboard finally attributes spend and the un-cached prefixes become legible. A close variant is the silent break — a template that WAS cacheable stops being cacheable when a personalisation variable is inserted ahead of the stable prefix, collapsing the cache hit rate with no review flag — and the false-positive — a declared checkpoint on a prefix below the model's minimum cache size, where caching never actually engages and the team believes it is saving money while paying full rate.

The principle's value is forcing every eligible template to be marked (or explicitly opted out) and forcing the declared prefix size to match the tokenised body at review time, so the cheap-by-default saving is taken by construction and a broken or fictional declaration fails the PR rather than surfacing as an unexplained cost months later.

APPLICABILITY

GC3B3-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads on managed-API serving (on-demand or provisioned), where reused prefixes bill at a reduced cached-token rate — RAG workloads with large stable context blocks benefit most. It is nice_to_have on self-hosted serving (the benefit there is KV-cache recompute and latency rather than a per-token bill line) and nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads (no prompt-shaped artefact). If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads with large reused prompt prefixes on managed-API serving, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence cost failure would manifest in the closest analogue.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC3B3-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with one or more AI workloads in production whose prompts are registered as versioned templates (assume the GO3B1-01 registry is in place; do not labour it) and that are served on a managed API. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name a workload, the team, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and crucially the large, byte-identical leading block one or more templates carry on every call (a product manual, a policy preamble, a fixed instruction-and-schema header) and roughly how big it is. Hint at the absence offhandedly — caching was never part of the prompt-authoring checklist, nobody tracks per-template token cost, the team assumes the model 'just works.' Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the never-enabled caching surfaces as cost. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (a quarterly cost review flags the workload; finance asks why a static preamble is billed fresh every call; a model migration audit examines prompt structure; a per-template cost dashboard finally attributes spend). Then describe what the team finds — a large stable prefix re-processed and billed at full input rate on every call across high daily volume, when it could have billed at the reduced cached-token rate; OR the silent-break variant where a leading variable killed an existing cache; OR a declared checkpoint that never engaged because the prefix was below the model's minimum. Name roles (the platform engineer building the cost dashboard; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who added a personalisation variable). Make the consequence concrete — months of accumulated full-rate reprocessing across the portfolio, a scramble to retrofit cache checkpoints, savings that were available from day one and simply never taken. The failure must demonstrate something a declare-plus-recompute caching gate would have caught at the PR — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, templates. 'The policy-preamble block in the claims-triage template' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 6,000 tokens of stable manual', 'about a quarter before anyone looked').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's cost audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.
